summary: This paper critically examines the current 'humanoid hype' in robotics through the lens of responsible robotics principles. Drawing on the recently published Roadmap for Responsible Robotics, the authors question whether 'responsible humanoids' is a contradiction in terms. They analyze how massive investment in humanoid robots, driven by technical advances in locomotion, cheaper manufacturing, and AI progress, creates a situation where public perception, media portrayals, and business narratives inflate expectations. The paper maps key responsible robotics concepts -- trust, justice and fairness, accountability, sustainability, and privacy -- to the specific challenges posed by humanoid development. It argues that humanoids' anthropomorphic appearance fosters uncalibrated trust, that their production reinforces global inequalities, that software immaturity creates accountability gaps, that their energy-intensive bipedal design conflicts with sustainability goals, and that their embodied sensors transform interaction into surveillance. The authors call on the HRI community to critically assess the societal implications of humanoid robotics.

title: A Roadmap for Responsible Robotics: Promoting Human Agency and Collaborative Efforts
summary: This paper presents the outcomes of the Dagstuhl Seminar on Responsible Robotics, bringing together researchers from robotics, computer science, social and cognitive sciences, and philosophy. It highlights sociotechnical challenges unique to robotic systems that go beyond responsible AI, including issues arising from robots' physical embodiment, autonomy, and integration into human life. The roadmap identifies key values (dignity, autonomy, privacy, safety, trust, fairness, accountability, sustainability) and maps responsibilities across stakeholders including universities, companies, governments, and civil society. It provides actionable steps for ensuring robotic systems co-evolve with human societies, promoting human agency and humane values, and serves as a foundation for collaborative efforts toward responsible robotics.

title: Socially Pertinent Robots in Gerontological Healthcare
summary: This paper presents the EU H2020 SPRING project, which developed and evaluated an autonomous social robot for gerontological healthcare. A full-sized ARI humanoid robot was deployed at the Broca day-care gerontology hospital in Paris to interact with patients suspected of having dementia and their companions. The system features a novel modular software architecture with 52 ROS nodes handling perception (self-localisation, human tracking, speech processing, behaviour analysis), conversation management, and non-verbal behaviour generation. The architecture is one of the first to fully adopt the ROS4HRI standard for multi-modal human-robot interaction. Through two waves of experiments involving over 60 participants, the study found that users are receptive to the technology, especially when the robot's perception and action skills are robust to environmental clutter and flexible enough to handle diverse interactions.

title: From Percepts to Semantics: A Multi-modal Saliency Map to Support Social Robots' Attention
summary: This paper introduces multi-modal saliency maps as a novel approach to model visual attention for social robots. Inspired by human attention mechanisms, the model integrates multiple attention sources -- low-level perceptual cues, semantic cues, and social cues -- into spatially-grounded 3D saliency representations. The framework is characterised by being multi-route-based (parallel attention processing units), input-agnostic (translating diverse inputs into uniform spatial saliency), and spatially grounded (lightweight 3D structure suitable for robot gaze control). The authors provide a mathematical formulation of the saliency-combining operator with formal properties (commutativity, associativity, identity, annihilator), an open-source ROS-based software implementation, and an evaluation comparing generated saliency maps with human attention patterns across three social interaction scenarios.

title: Are You an Expert? Instruction Adaptation Using Multi-Modal Affect Detections with Thermal Imaging and Context
Fusion in Context: A Multimodal Approach to Affective State Recognition
Personalised Explanations in Long-term Human-Robot Interactions
TIAGo Head: an AI Powered Platform for Social Robotics
summary: This paper presents TIAGo Head, a new tabletop social robot developed by PAL Robotics. The robot is a compact (45cm, 5.75kg), cost-effective derivative of the full-sized TIAGo Pro, focused exclusively on social interaction. It features an RGB-D camera, 4-microphone array, speakers, a 10-inch touchscreen face with expressive LCD eyes, and 2 degrees of freedom for pan-tilt head motion. The software architecture runs on ROS 2 and integrates the ROS4HRI framework for social perception, local LLM inference (via Ollama) for natural conversation, an OWL2-based knowledge base for situated reasoning, and the reMap framework for spatial semantics. A use-case demonstration shows TIAGo Head serving as an airport receptionist, displaying news and conversing with travelers using its mixed-reality interaction simulator.

title: Dynamics of Mental Models: Objective vs. Subjective User Understanding of a Robot in the Wild
Acceptability and Usability of a Socially Assistive Robot Integrated With a Large Language Model for Enhanced Human-Robot Interaction in a Geriatric Care Institution: Mixed Methods Evaluation
summary: This study evaluates the acceptability and usability of a social assistive robot enhanced with a large language model (LLM) in a geriatric day-care hospital. Conducted at the Broca hospital in Paris between May 2023 and July 2024, the study involved 97 participants (65 patients and 32 informal caregivers) across three experimental waves using the PAL Robotics ARI robot. Participants interacted spontaneously with the robot and then completed standardised acceptability and usability questionnaires. Results showed significant increases in both acceptability and usability scores across the three waves, with qualitative analysis revealing greater comfort and satisfaction particularly after the integration of an LLM, which enabled more natural, coherent, and context-sensitive dialogues. The findings highlight the value of iterative, user-centred refinement when integrating social robots in geriatric care.

title: VDB-based Spatially Grounded Semantics for Interactive Robots
summary: This paper presents a novel approach for representing spatially-grounded semantics in interactive robots using VDB (Volumetric Dynamic B+tree) data structures. The method combines spatial and symbolic data by using a voxel-based structure optimised for dynamic and sparse information, along with a global lookup table to manage and track spatially-grounded entities and their relationships. The system maps 3D regions of space to semantic entities stored in an RDF-based knowledge base, enabling queries through semantic web APIs such as SPARQL. Implemented as an extension of the reMap ROS 2 package, the framework supports real-time insertion and deletion of semantic instances with algorithms of complexity O(p*a). Initial tests on healthcare and hospital environment datasets demonstrate efficient real-time spatial grounding of semantic information.

title: Personalised Explainable Robots Using LLMs
Demonstration of an Open-source ROS 2 Framework and Simulator for Situated Interactive Social Robot
Hands-on: From Zero to an Interactive Social Robot using ROS4HRI and LLMs
reMap: Spatially-grounded and Queryable Semantics for Interactive Robots
summary: This paper introduces reMap, an open-source framework for the efficient representation, storage, and retrieval of spatially-grounded semantics for interactive robots. reMap formalises Representation Maps (RMs) as three-dimensional functions that each represent a different type of semantic information in space, and Representation Map Sources (RMSs) that transform robot percepts into these maps. The framework defines operators for combining stored maps and extracting new spatially-grounded semantic information, and includes a SPARQL-based query language for programmatic retrieval. The implementation uses dense voxel maps based on the high-performance OpenVDB format, integrated into ROS. Compared to existing approaches, reMap uniquely supports 3D dense fields, human modelling, spatial reasoning, and a spatial API, all within a modular architecture.

title: Context Matters: Understanding Socially Appropriate Affective Responses Via Sentence Embeddings
summary: This paper presents a novel methodology for context-aware affect recognition in human-robot interaction using sentence embeddings. The approach leverages pre-trained language models to distinguish between socially appropriate and inappropriate interactions by measuring the semantic distance between facial expression descriptions (generated by GPT-4V) and predefined contrasting reference points in an embedding space. The method is evaluated on both the MMI Facial Expression Database and an in-the-wild dataset collected during interactions with a Furhat robot in a university library. Results show that classification performance (F1 scores) is highest when using the correct context, demonstrating that contextual factors are essential for effective social interaction understanding and appropriate robot responses.

title: Co-designing Explainable Robots: A Participatory Design Approach for HRI
summary: This paper proposes a participatory design (PD) framework for co-designing explainable robots in human-robot interaction. Rather than adding explanations as extra features to existing systems, the approach advocates designing explainability from the beginning through user involvement. The framework consists of three stages: gathering initial information, in-situ co-design (comprising initial prototyping, multi-modal interaction design, and behaviour fine-tuning), and evaluation. A key tool is the 'interaction table' that maps potential intuitiveness and understandability issues to explainability measures, integrated with the IEEE P7001 transparency standard. The framework is demonstrated through a use case in the SAFE-LY project, where a robot was co-designed with geriatric unit staff for tasks including patrolling, deliveries, and remote inspection.

summary: This paper introduces social embeddings, a compact, semantics-preserving mathematical representation of social situations for robots. The approach automatically generates a textual description of a robot's social environment using perception capabilities (tracking positions, gaze directions, motion, and speech) and converts it into a vector representation via pre-trained large language model text embeddings. The paper presents the algorithm for generating scene descriptors, analyses key properties of these embeddings including their ability to measure social similarity, and demonstrates their operationalisation on actual social robots. The work argues that social embeddings provide a quantitative pseudo-metric for social situations, enabling robots to compare and recognise social scenarios they encounter, with potential applications in social-aware navigation and interaction management.

title: Measuring Visual Social Engagement from Proxemics and Gaze in the Real World
summary: This paper extends prior work on measuring Visual Social Engagement (VSE) from virtual online settings to real-world scenarios, aiming to improve social robots' situational awareness. The VSE metric combines two social cues -- interpersonal proximity and mutual gaze -- to quantify engagement between individuals. Using the SoGrIn dataset of 30 participants in 6 groups performing collaborative and social tasks, the authors had human annotators identify interaction start points rather than relying on arbitrary distance thresholds. They computed VSE scores and constructed individual interaction profiles for each participant. Results showed that while the metric could capture engagement dynamics in specific frames, applying it to real-world data revealed high variability in interaction profiles. Correlations between personality traits (Big Five) and engagement metrics were weak and not statistically robust. The authors conclude that further refinement is needed, including incorporating facial action units and speech detection, before the VSE metric can reliably assess in-person group interactions.

title: Probabilistic fusion of persons' body features: the Mr. Potato algorithm
summary: This paper presents the 'Mr. Potato' algorithm for probabilistically fusing multiple detected social features (faces, bodies, voices) into complete person models on social robots. In multi-party interactions, independent perception modules detect different features that must be matched to form coherent person representations. The algorithm builds a persons-features graph where edges represent probabilistic associations between detected features and known persons. It then searches all possible partitions of this graph to find the optimal grouping that maximizes overall affinity while respecting constraints: each person has at most one feature of each type, and features not yet associated with known persons are assigned to anonymous persons. The algorithm also maintains stability by computing indirect association edges when direct detection is lost. Formalised within the ROS4HRI standard (ROS REP-155), the open-source C++ implementation runs on PAL Robotics' social robots in real-time.

title: Dataset and Evaluation of Automatic Speech Recognition for Multi-lingual Intent Recognition on Social Robots
summary: This paper presents a multilingual dataset and systematic evaluation of Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems for social robots. The authors recorded voice commands from 17 adult participants (plus 3 children and 1 elderly person) in 5 European languages (English, Spanish, Italian, French, Catalan) using PAL Robotics' ARI robot in two configurations: with and without body covers over the microphone. They benchmarked four ASR systems -- Google Speech, OpenWhisper, Vosk, and NVidia Riva -- evaluating Word Error Rate and downstream intent/entity recognition using the Rasa framework. Results showed that cloud-based systems like Google Speech achieved the best overall performance, but open-source alternatives like Vosk performed competitively on constrained compute budgets. All ASRs performed poorly on Catalan. Intent recognition via Rasa achieved 80-90% accuracy across ASRs, partially compensating for transcription errors. The dataset is publicly available.

title: Placing by Touching: An empirical study on the importance of tactile sensing for precise object placing
summary: This paper investigates the use of tactile sensing for stable object placement on flat surfaces, proposing a learning-based approach that does not require visual feedback. A neural network takes input from piezo-resistive Myrmex tactile sensors (16x16 taxel arrays) mounted on a TIAGo robot's parallel gripper, and optionally force/torque data, to predict a corrective rotation matrix that aligns the object's placing normal with the table surface. The architecture uses convolutional layers for tactile data processing followed by an MLP. Trained on only 1600 samples from two primitive 3D-printed objects, the tactile-only model achieved 90-100% placement success on training objects and generalised impressively to novel everyday objects unseen during training. The tactile-based approach significantly outperformed force/torque-only models (15-20% success) and performed comparably to an oracle motion-capture baseline, demonstrating that tactile sensing provides crucial contact information for dexterous manipulation.

title: Multi-modal Affect Detection Using Thermal and Optical Imaging in a Gamified Robotic Exercise

summary: This paper examines multi-modal affect detection using thermal imaging and facial action units during a gamified robotic exercise designed for upper-limb rehabilitation. Thirty participants played a tangible Pacman-like game using Cellulo robots across four phases designed to induce specific emotional states: familiarisation, enjoyment, frustration, and boredom. Data was collected via thermal cameras and GoPro cameras capturing facial expressions. Three machine learning models were compared: LSTM, Gaussian Naive Bayes, and a combined approach. The multimodal combination of thermal imaging and action units with LSTM achieved the highest accuracy of 77% for four-class emotion classification over 7-second sliding windows. Thermal imaging alone showed the lowest standard deviation among participants, suggesting more consistent detection. The results indicate that thermal imaging and action units offer a promising non-intrusive method for recognising affective states in healthcare robotics applications such as gamified therapy.

title: Challenges of deploying assistive robots in real-life scenarios: an industrial perspective
summary: This paper presents an industrial perspective from PAL Robotics on the challenges of deploying assistive social robots for elderly care in real-world settings. It identifies key needs of the aging population -- cognitive support, physical support, reducing loneliness, and reducing caregiver burden -- and describes how ARI and TIAGo robots have been deployed across multiple European projects (SHAPES, ALMI, AMIBA, SPRING, DIH HERO SANDRO, NHoA). Practical lessons include: cognitive games must be validated by psychologists early on; personalisation and flexibility are essential; safety is prioritised over accuracy by end users; caregivers need easy voice-based control interfaces; and non-medical social activities matter as much as medical ones. Technical deployment challenges include navigating cluttered home environments, adapting maps with virtual obstacles, and implementing the ROS4HRI standard for multi-user perception. The paper emphasises the gap between lab prototypes and robust real-world deployment.

title: SoGrIn: a non-verbal dataset of social group-level interactions
summary: This paper introduces the Social Group Interactions (SoGrIn) dataset, a publicly available collection of non-verbal social signals captured during group-level interactions. Thirty participants, organised into 6 groups of 5, completed a competitive game involving collaborative and individual social tasks across four rooms designed to elicit different interaction types (discussions, collaborative jigsaws, human-robot interaction with a NAO robot, and debates). The dataset includes precise proxemics data from a VICON motion capture system, facial features from helmet-mounted GoPro cameras (facial landmarks, gaze direction, facial action units), Big Five personality questionnaire responses, and demographic information, totalling approximately 60 minutes of recordings. The dataset fills a gap in existing corpora by combining accurate motion-capture positioning with detailed facial information during naturalistic group social interactions, making it valuable for social signal understanding and social robotics research.

title: ROS4HRI: Standardising an Interface for Human-Robot Interaction
venue: HRI -- Workshop Advancing HRI Research and Benchmarking Through Open-Source Ecosystems
summary: This paper presents ROS4HRI, an open-source standard for Human-Robot Interaction built on top of the Robot Operating System (ROS). It addresses the lack of standardised interfaces for social perception in HRI by defining a common framework for representing humans through four identifiers: face, body, voice, and person IDs. Transient feature IDs are distinguished from permanent person IDs, enabling robust tracking across detection losses. The standard specifies a ROS topic structure under the /humans namespace, URDF-based human kinematic models with 15 body links, TF frame conventions for spatial representation, and custom ROS messages for social signals including facial landmarks, action units, expressions, gaze, gestures, engagement, and speech. The paper also introduces initial work on standardising user intents as abstract descriptions of operations requested by users. ROS4HRI is implemented on PAL Robotics' ARI robot and aims to improve benchmarking and reproducibility in the HRI community.

title: Unifying Bottom-Up and Top-Down Attention Models for Social Robots
venue: HRI -- Workshop Semantic Scene Understanding for Human-Robot Interaction
summary: This extended abstract proposes a unified data-driven model for social robot overt visual attention that combines both bottom-up (perceptual) and top-down (semantic) processes. Current approaches to robot gaze behaviour are either biologically inspired (bottom-up only), heuristics-based (top-down only, narrow applicability), or data-driven (limited to specific semantic features). The authors argue that effective social robot gaze must integrate both perceptual saliency and semantic understanding. They recorded a dataset of 11 video clips of office interactions and crowd-sourced ground-truth gaze attention data from 60 online participants using WebGazer eye tracking. Their proposed architecture concatenates semantic segmentation information with ConvLSTM-based perceptual saliency features, feeding them into an MLP to generate probability distributions for the robot's next gaze target.

title: Open-source Natural Language Processing on the PAL Robotics ARI Social Robot
summary: This paper demonstrates an open-source natural language processing (NLP) pipeline for the PAL Robotics ARI social robot. The pipeline integrates state-of-the-art open-source tools for automatic speech recognition (Vosk) and dialogue management (Rasa), replacing previous cloud-based solutions that raised privacy concerns and required reliable internet connectivity. The ROS-based system implements the ROS4HRI standard and features full ROS compatibility, internationalisation support, a dedicated language centre for managing translations and model swapping, and user intent recognition decoupled from the application controller. The pipeline also integrates with a Knowledge Base and Reasoning framework for multi-modal dialogue management. The demonstration targets assistive robotics and elderly care domains, where the ARI robot has been deployed in hospitals and care centres through several EU-funded projects including H2020 SHAPES, SPRING, TALBOT, and PRO-CARED.

title: “It’s Important to Think of Pepper as a Teaching Aid or Resource External to the Classroom”: A Social Robot in a School for Autistic Children
summary: This paper reports on a three-week deployment of a Softbank Pepper robot in a UK Special Educational Needs (SEN) school for autistic children. The robot's behaviours and integration were co-designed with children and teachers, focusing on improving pupil well-being rather than cognitive outcomes. Using a mixed-method approach, the study investigated what underpins successful integration of a social robot in a SEN school ecosystem and whether the robot can deliver a net gain for children's well-being. The robot was placed in school corridors for unstructured, child-led interactions rather than controlled one-to-one sessions. Results showed the robot maintained a steady level of interactions (330 interactions, 16 hours over 3 weeks) with a small but meaningful group of children, and had a positive impact on their well-being. The interdisciplinary team spanned education, robotics, and architecture, following the UNICEF ethical framework for AI and children.

title: Deliberative Democracy with Robot Swarms
summary: This paper investigates whether a swarm of small mobile robots (called MOSAIX Tiles) can facilitate group decision-making in the context of deliberative democracy. The Tiles are 4-inch touchscreen-on-wheels robots that act as physical avatars for participants' opinions, visually representing the diversity of views in a group. A user study with 46 participants (groups of 7-8) compared two conditions: a moving condition where robots travelled and mixed randomly after opinions were entered, and a stationary condition where each participant held a Tile like a smartphone. Results showed participants felt comfortable using the robots and that robot movement significantly increased user engagement (more interactions per participant) and led to greater opinion change between first and last entries. The study found that seeing others' opinions on moving robots had a statistically significant impact on individual decision-making, suggesting swarm robotics can effectively support democratic group processes.

title: Bio-Inspired Grasping Controller for Sensorized 2-DoF Grippers
summary: This paper presents a bio-inspired grasping controller for sensorised 2-DoF parallel grippers, evaluated on the TIAGo service robot. The controller combines free-space position control with in-contact force control, modelled after human grasping phases: finger closing, establishing contact, and holding. Tactile fingertip sensors detect first contact, at which point individual finger joints pause to minimise object displacement until force-closure is achieved. During holding, the controller maintains a desired grasp force while being compliant with external forces and explicitly compensating for gravity to prevent object drift. The controller decouples internal and external force control using a PI controller for force regulation and a P controller for external force compliance. Experiments show the controller reduces object displacement to less than 1mm for sufficiently heavy objects compared to standard position control, and ablation studies demonstrate the utility of each component.

title: Automatic Engagement Detection for Social Situation Assessment
venue: UKRAS22 Conference “Robotics for Unconstrained Environments” Proceedings
summary: This short paper proposes collecting an in-person dataset of dynamic group interactions to model social engagement for improving robots' social awareness. Building on previous work that created a visual social engagement metric from an online game using proxemics and mutual gaze, the authors aim to enrich the metric with additional social signals captured in person. The planned dataset includes both human-human and human-robot group interactions, with participants completing tasks that encourage forming groups of 2-5 people. A Pepper robot controlled via Wizard-of-Oz is included to capture human-robot interactions. Data collection uses a VICON motion capture system for proximity and gaze, plus GoPro cameras for facial and audio recordings. The authors plan to develop a model that automatically detects engagement levels of individuals and groups, ultimately improving social robots' ability to understand and integrate into social scenarios.

title: Measuring Visual Social Engagement from Proxemics and Gaze
summary: This paper introduces a novel visual social engagement metric derived from two social signals: proxemics (interpersonal distance) and mutual gaze. Data was collected from 88 participants playing a 3D online murder-mystery game built in Godot, where players interacted with NPC groups. The metric is defined as mutual gaze divided by distance between participants, creating a score between 0 and 1. Using this metric, the authors generated unique interaction profiles for each participant by averaging engagement scores over interaction windows. Results showed a wide distribution of interaction profiles, suggesting participants have distinct interaction styles that are fairly consistent across their interactions. The authors investigated correlations between these profiles and Big Five personality traits but found no strong correlations. The work aims to ultimately improve social robots' situational awareness by providing a computationally lightweight engagement measure based on easily observable social signals.

title: Automatic Frustration Detection Using Thermal Imaging
summary: This paper investigates whether thermal imaging can detect frustration during human-robot interaction. The authors collected data from 18 participants experiencing two types of frustration induced by a NAO robot: failure-induced frustration (a trivia quiz where the robot intentionally gave wrong answers) and cognitive load-induced frustration (a dual coding and mental rotation task). Features were extracted from thermal images (facial ROIs: nose, forehead, cheek, lower lip), RGB images (action units), and electrodermal activity (EDA). Using a K-Nearest Neighbors classifier with Sequential Forward Floating Selection for feature selection, the model achieved 89% accuracy with RGB features, 87% with thermal features alone, 84% with EDA, and 86% with all modalities combined. The work demonstrates that thermal imaging is a viable non-intrusive alternative for detecting frustration in real-time HRI scenarios.

title: Towards using Behaviour Trees for Long-term Social Robot Behaviour
summary: This paper introduces a Behaviour Tree (BT) based approach for designing long-term social robot behaviour on the PAL Robotics ARI robot, developed within the EU H2020 SHAPES project for elderly care. Two specific behaviours are implemented: an idle behaviour where the robot waits at a docking station and initiates interaction when a person approaches (via face detection, touch, or speech), and a reminder-delivery behaviour where the robot actively navigates to points of interest, identifies a specific user through face recognition, and delivers personalised reminders. The paper details the BT structure including custom nodes for face identification, text-to-speech, web actions, and navigation. The authors argue that BTs offer advantages over finite state machines for social robotics due to their modularity, reactivity, and extensibility, though they note limitations for highly variable or complex scenarios.

title: Kinematically-consistent Real-time 3D Human Body Estimation for Physical and Social HRI
summary: This paper presents a ROS-integrated software tool for real-time, kinematically consistent 3D human body estimation, designed for physical and social human-robot interaction. The system uses Google Mediapipe's holistic model to infer 3D skeleton poses from RGB images, then combines these with a URDF-defined human kinematic model from the ROS4HRI framework. Joint angles are extracted via inverse kinematics and published as ROS tf frames and joint state messages. An optional RGB-D camera enables absolute 3D positioning. The kinematic model ensures anatomical consistency by enforcing joint rotation limits, preventing estimation of physically impossible poses caused by self-occlusions. Running at approximately 8Hz on CPU without GPU acceleration, the system produces flowing and realistic body model movements. Current limitations include single-person detection only and lack of height personalisation.

title: LEADOR: A Method for End-To-End Participatory Design of Autonomous Social Robots
summary: This paper presents LEADOR (Led-by-Experts Automation and Design Of Robots), an end-to-end participatory design methodology for autonomous social robots. LEADOR extends traditional participatory design by replacing the offline programming or Wizard-of-Oz phase with an in-situ teaching phase where domain experts (e.g., teachers, therapists) directly teach the robot how to behave in real-time within the actual interaction context. The method comprises five stages: problem definition, interaction design, system specification (co-designing state/action spaces and teaching interfaces), technical implementation, and real-world deployment with online teaching. This approach captures experts' implicit knowledge and supports mutual shaping between the robot and its context of use. The methodology is derived from two foundational studies: an educational robot for school children and a robot fitness coach. LEADOR is agnostic to the specific learning algorithm used and aims to produce robots that can operate autonomously in sensitive environments while maintaining responsible design practices.

title: UNICEF Guidance on AI for Children: Application to the Design of a Social Robot For and With Autistic Children
venue: The Roles of Robotics in Achieving the UN's Social Development Goals -- IROS2021 workshop
summary: This paper describes a project embedding a Softbank Pepper robot in a UK Special Educational Needs (SEN) school for three weeks to support the well-being of autistic children. The authors use the nine UNICEF Policy Guidelines on AI for Children as a framework to systematically evaluate their participatory design methodology. The robot's behaviours (storytelling, dancing, playing music, cuddling, telling jokes) were co-designed with the children and teachers through focus groups and workshops. The study prioritised autistic voice and child-led, unstructured interactions in school corridors rather than scheduled classroom sessions. Each of the nine UNICEF principles is addressed: supporting well-being, ensuring inclusion, fairness, data privacy (no personal data stored, robot offline), physical and psychological safety, transparency, empowering stakeholders, preparing children for AI, and creating an enabling environment. This is argued to be the first systematic consideration of the UNICEF AI guidelines in the context of social robotics for autistic children.

title: ROS for Human-Robot Interaction
summary: This paper introduces ROS4HRI, a set of conventions and standard interfaces for the Robot Operating System (ROS) designed to facilitate human-robot interaction. The authors address the lack of a standard human representation model in robotics by proposing a multi-modal framework built on four identifiers: face, body, voice, and person. Each identifier has different persistence properties -- face, body, and voice IDs are transient, while person IDs are permanent. The framework specifies a ROS topic structure under the /humans namespace with sub-namespaces for faces, bodies, voices, persons, and interactions. It defines new ROS message types (hri_msgs) and TF frame conventions for spatially representing humans, including a URDF-based kinematic model with 15 links and 18 joints generated on-the-fly from observed height. The open-source reference implementation covers 3D tracking and matching of skeletons and faces in groups of up to 10 people.

title: The Effectiveness of Dynamically Processed Incremental Descriptions in Human Robot Interaction
summary: This paper explores dynamically processed incremental referring descriptions for human-robot interaction in a nuclear decommissioning scenario. The authors build a system that generates initially ambiguous spatial descriptions of objects (barrels), then incrementally refines them using linguistic repair statements based on the user's actions as they remotely pilot a robot. This dynamic approach is compared against static non-ambiguous descriptions that uniquely identify targets upfront. In a study with 31 participants remotely operating a PAL Robotics TIAGo robot to sort coloured barrels, the dynamic system proved significantly more efficient, taking only 62% of the time on average to find the correct barrel. Participants also preferred the dynamic system. The work is situated in the context of UK nuclear decommissioning at the Sellafield site, where robots must operate in hazardous radiation-hot environments with human-in-the-loop control.

title: Predicting Human Interactivity State from Surrounding Social Signals
summary: This paper investigates whether a person's interactivity state in a group can be predicted from the social signals of surrounding group members rather than from the target person's own signals. Using a dataset of 6 participants playing the social game Mafia (with 3 in frame), the authors extract features including head distances, head angles, facial action units, emotions, and body poses. A multi-layer perceptron model achieves 60% accuracy using the target person's own social signals, but 65% accuracy when using only the surrounding group members' signals. Post-hoc factor analysis reveals that facial action units of surrounding members and the distance between the target person and group members are the most important features. The preliminary results suggest that peer influence and group dynamics carry meaningful information about individual interactivity.

title: Framing the Challenge of Social Interaction Modelling: One Case Study
summary: This short paper frames the challenge of social interaction modelling for HRI by presenting a case study from a recorded group interaction. The authors argue that social interactions are complex, dynamic, and difficult to formalise as a 'wicked problem.' Using a snapshot of three people playing Mafia, they contrast what humans can perceive (expressions, gaze, mood, social dynamics) with what a robot's sensors would capture, highlighting significant gaps in current robotic social cognition. The paper emphasises the dynamic nature of social situations and argues that current approaches that interpret single frames lose critical temporal information. The authors call for better algorithms to detect observable social behaviours, improved representations of human behaviour and cognition, and acknowledgment that internal states remain inherently unobservable, making complete modelling impossible.

title: Towards Real World Impacts: Design, Development, and Deployment of Social Robots in the Wild
An Agency-Directed Approach to Test Generation for Simulation-based Autonomous Vehicle Verification
summary: This paper proposes an agency-directed approach to test generation for simulation-based autonomous vehicle (AV) verification. Using multi-agent systems with belief-desire-intention (BDI) agents implemented in Jason, the authors give agency to pedestrian test agents that strategically interact with the AV to trigger collision-avoidance assertions. The case study involves a simulated two-lane road where pedestrian agents attempt to enter the AV's precondition zone. Two agency-directed behaviours are compared against random baselines: 'proximity' (cross when AV is near) and 'election' (only the nearest agent crosses). Results show that a single directed agent outperforms a random agent by more than 2:1 in generating successful tests. The directed agents also produce higher test scores indicating more realistic behaviour. The approach generates twice as many effective tests as pseudo-random methods while providing more realistic driving scenarios.

title: Performing Human-Robot Interaction User Studies in Virtual Reality
summary: This study investigates whether virtual reality (VR) can serve as a platform for conducting human-robot interaction user studies by replicating a real-world experiment in VR. The original study by Kahn et al. examined whether participants would keep a robot tour guide's secret. In the VR replication, 38 participants experienced a virtual museum tour with either a social or non-social Robovie robot guide using an HTC Vive headset. Results showed notable differences from the original: participants kept the secret more often while in VR (50-60%) than after removing the headset (25-33%). The environment significantly influenced secret-keeping behaviour, but the robot's social vs. non-social behaviour did not. A positive correlation was found between perceiving the robot as a social being and tendency to keep its secret. The authors conclude that more work is needed to determine VR's suitability for HRI experiments.

title: In-Situ Learning from a Domain Expert for Real World Socially Assistive Robot Deployment
summary: This paper presents a framework for in-situ, online interactive machine learning (IML) to enable domain experts to be involved at every stage of socially assistive robot design and automation. The application is a Pepper robot acting as an exercise coach for the NHS Couch to 5km (C25K) running programme. A fitness instructor co-designed the robot's action space over 6 sessions (12.5 hours), defining task actions (run/walk commands) and social support actions (encouragement, humour, sympathy) with style modifiers. The system uses a dual learner architecture -- one for action type, one for style -- operating over a 20-dimensional input space including task state, performance metrics, heart rate, engagement measures, and personality traits. The IML approach has two phases: supervised (expert teleoperates and validates) and autonomous (robot acts independently), allowing the expert to progressively transfer control as the robot learns appropriate personalised behaviour.

title: The Unexpected Daily Situations (UDS) Dataset: A New Benchmark for Socially-Aware Assistive Robots
summary: This paper presents the Unexpected Daily Situations (UDS) dataset, a benchmark for developing socially-aware assistive robots. The dataset consists of short video recordings (1-3 minutes each) of everyday scenarios where unexpected events occur -- such as someone tripping, burning themselves, or dropping objects -- in familiar indoor environments like kitchens and living rooms. Recorded using a TIAGo robot with RGB-D sensor, stereo microphone, and 2D LIDAR, the scenarios are categorised into four types: situations requiring assistance due to perspective limitations, physical capability limitations, task-related situations where help improves the outcome, and non-task-related situations. The benchmark aims to push the state of the art in spatio-temporal, semantic, and social modelling by requiring robots to infer human intentions, detect errors, and select appropriate repair actions. Crowd-sourced annotations via Amazon Mechanical Turk provide ground truth for intention recognition and assistive behaviour baselines.

title: Towards using Virtual Reality for Replicating HRI Studies
summary: This short paper presents an initial attempt to use Virtual Reality (VR) for replicating HRI studies, specifically replicating a trust-related experiment by Kahn et al. where participants decided whether to keep a robot tour guide's secret. The VR version used Unreal Engine 4 with a virtual museum setting and HTC Vive headset. With 38 participants, results differed from the original: 60% kept the secret in VR for the non-social robot versus 50% for the social robot. After leaving VR, only 25% and 33% kept the secret respectively. Participants rated both robot conditions higher on Mental Emotional and Social Other scales than in the original study. The authors note too many confounding factors to draw definitive conclusions about VR's suitability for replicating HRI experiments, but emphasise the importance of study replication in the field.

title: Couch to 5km Robot Coach: An Autonomous, Human-Trained Socially Assistive Robot
summary: This paper presents a robot exercise coach, co-designed with and trained by a human fitness instructor using interactive machine learning (IML), to support the UK NHS Couch to     5km (C25K) running programme. The programme consists of 3x weekly exercise sessions over 9 weeks, building from short walk/run intervals to 30 minutes of running. The robot's action space includes     task actions (walk, run) and social support actions (praise, dialogue, check-ups, sympathy, animated movement, proximity adjustments). A KNN-based IML algorithm learns from the instructor's     demonstrations and feedback in real time. The system was evaluated in a university gym with 10 participants over 3 months, comparing heuristic, supervised ML, and autonomous ML conditions. Results     show the autonomous system learned personalised behaviour that replicated the instructor's action distributions, demonstrating that human-trained interactive machine learning is a viable approach for     generating autonomous socially assistive robot behaviour.

title: Teaching robots social autonomy from in situ human guidance
summary: This paper evaluates SPARC (Supervised Progressively Autonomous Robot Competencies), a framework for teaching robots social autonomy through in situ human guidance. SPARC     addresses the challenge of balancing robot autonomy with human control in sensitive domains like education and therapy. The robot starts as a blank slate, teleoperated by a human teacher, and     progressively learns autonomous behaviour using online machine learning. The teacher can demonstrate actions, confirm or override robot suggestions, and provide corrections, all feeding into a     continuously improving policy. The approach is evaluated in a field study where 8-to-10-year-old children learn about food webs through a touchscreen educational game, with a robot tutor guided by a     human teacher. SPARC satisfies key requirements: the robot exhibits acceptable behaviour from the start, learns quickly from limited demonstrations, and integrates naturally into human workflows     without requiring technical expertise from the teacher.

title: Generating Spatial Referring Expressions in a Social Robot: Dynamic vs Non-Ambiguous
summary: This paper presents a method for generating dynamic spatial referring expressions for human-robot interaction, contrasting them with traditional non-ambiguous     descriptions. While most algorithms aim to produce fully unambiguous descriptions, humans naturally give under-specified descriptions and rely on repair strategies to narrow down the referent. The     authors propose a system that generates an initial ambiguous description and then dynamically provides follow-up repair statements (negate, elaborate, or positive feedback) based on the user's     actions, using a machine learning classifier. A study with 61 participants in a city-planning game on a tabletop touchscreen compared dynamic versus non-ambiguous descriptions. The MLP classifier     achieved 96.5% accuracy for categorizing interaction states. Results demonstrate that the dynamic method can be more efficient for identifying locations compared to non-ambiguous descriptions, even     in a task environment biased toward favouring non-ambiguous communication.

title: Simulation-based physics reasoning for consistent scene estimation in an HRI context
summary: This paper presents a simulation-based physics reasoning component for building consistent scene estimations in human-robot interaction contexts. Integrated into the     Underworlds situation-assessment framework, it uses the Bullet RT physics engine to stabilize object poses, infer positions of out-of-sight objects, and recognize human actions in real time. The     system addresses common perception challenges in tabletop HRI settings where objects may appear to float, overlap, or disappear due to occlusions. The physics reasoner computes predicates such as     isPhysicallyPlausible and isPerceived for each object, derives allocentric spatial relations (isOnTop, isIn), and infers human actions (Pick, Place, Release) by detecting physical inconsistencies.     The architecture follows a client/server model with providers, filters, monitors, and readers as modular components. Experiments on a PR2 robot with a Kinect2 sensor demonstrate the system's ability     to handle challenging situations including occluded objects and complex manipulation scenarios.

title: What Can You See? Identifying Cues on Internal States from the Kinematics of Natural Social Interactions
summary: This paper investigates whether internal states and social constructs can be identified from the movements of people in natural social interactions. Using video clips from     the PInSoRo dataset of children interacting around a touchscreen table, participants on Amazon Mechanical Turk viewed either full-scene clips or movement-alone clips (stick-figure representations         extracted using OpenPose). They rated seven constructs: boredom, aggression, cooperation, dominance, aimless play, fun, and excitement. Results show that while full-scene clips yielded higher         inter-rater agreement, movement-alone data still produced above-chance agreement, indicating that internal states are identifiable from movement information alone. Factor analysis revealed three         consistent constructs across conditions: interaction imbalance, interaction valence, and engagement. Machine learning classifiers trained on full-scene ratings performed similarly when tested on         movement-alone ratings, supporting the feasibility of robots using low-dimensional positional data to decode social states.

title: On the Impact of Different Types of Errors on Trust in Human-Robot Interaction: Are laboratory-based HRI experiments trustworthy?
summary: This paper investigates the impact of different types of robot errors on trust in human-robot interaction through two independent laboratory studies with 160 total         participants. The studies examine whether technical failures versus decision-level failures (social norm violations) differentially affect trust, measured through both behavioural (proxemics) and         subjective (questionnaire) metrics. Two robotic platforms were used: Aldebaran's Pepper and PAL's TIAGo, guiding participants through assembly tasks. While the authors found a weak general effect of         errors on reported and observed trust levels, no significant differences between error types were found in either study. The paper discusses this negative result in light of the experimental         protocols used and argues that laboratory-based HRI experiments may have fundamental limitations in assessing trust ecologically. The authors advocate for the community to move toward alternative         methodologies for measuring trust in more naturalistic settings.

title: Towards Generating Spatial Referring Expressions in a Social Robot: Dynamic vs Non-Ambiguous
summary: This short paper presents preliminary work toward a dynamic method of generating spatial referring expressions for social robots. While humans naturally use ambiguous,         under-specified descriptions and repair strategies in conversation, previous artificial generation methods have focused on producing non-ambiguous descriptions. The authors propose a system that         initially produces ambiguous spatial descriptions and then dynamically generates repair statements (negate, elaborate, or positive feedback) based on the listener's actions. Data was collected from         18 participants playing a city-planning game on an interactive tabletop, where pairs took turns as describer and manipulator. An SVC classifier was trained on 4701 datapoints, achieving 89% accuracy         but struggling with negation classification. Two follow-up pilot studies (N=8, N=9) compared the dynamic approach with non-ambiguous robot descriptions, yielding mixed results that informed further         refinement of the classifier and timing mechanisms.

title: Effective Persuasion Strategies for Socially Assistive Robots
summary: This paper investigates whether human persuasion strategies can increase the effectiveness of socially assistive robots (SARs). Grounded in the Elaboration Likelihood         Model (ELM), the study examines three persuasive strategies: citing expertise (S1), displaying goodwill (S2), and emphasising similarity (S3) between robot and participant. A 4-condition         between-subject Wizard-of-Oz study with 92 participants used a Pepper robot guiding an open-ended wrist exercise task. Robot persuasiveness was measured objectively by the number of exercise         repetitions completed and exercise duration. Results show that displays of goodwill and similarity significantly increased robot persuasiveness as measured by participant behaviour. However, these         strategies had no impact on subjective measures of robot credibility or likeability, and subjective perception of the robot did not correlate with objective behavioural measures. The authors suggest         this disconnect highlights difficulties in accurately measuring perception of robots using subjective measures.

title: The PInSoRo dataset: Supporting the data-driven study of child-child and child-robot social dynamics
bibtex:
@article{lemaignan2018pinsoro,
author = {Lemaignan, Séverin AND Edmunds, Charlotte E. R. AND Senft, Emmanuel AND Belpaeme, Tony},
journal = {PLOS ONE},
publisher = {Public Library of Science},
title = {The PInSoRo dataset: Supporting the data-driven study of child-child and child-robot social dynamics},
year = {2018},

url = {https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0205999},
pages = {1-19},
number = {10},
doi = {10.1371/journal.pone.0205999}}
summary: This paper introduces the PInSoRo dataset, a novel open dataset designed to support data-driven study of child-child and child-robot social dynamics. The dataset         comprises over 45 hours of hand-coded recordings of social interactions between 45 child-child pairs and 30 child-robot pairs engaged in free-play on a large interactive touchscreen. The free-play         sandbox paradigm allows children to freely draw and manipulate items without explicit goals, eliciting rich, natural social behaviours. The dataset includes fully calibrated video recordings, 3D face         recordings, skeletal information, audio recordings, game interaction logs, and manual annotations of social constructs. Two experimental conditions are established: child-child interactions as a         baseline for natural social dynamics, and child-asocial robot interactions where the robot plays autonomously but avoids all social engagement. The dataset is designed to enable machine learning         approaches for both analysis and synthesis of complex social dynamics in HRI.

title: Using a Robot Peer to Encourage the Production of Spatial Concepts in a Second Language
summary: This paper investigates whether a robot peer can effectively encourage the production of spatial concepts in a second language (L2) by children, compared to a human         experimenter. A study with 25 English-speaking children aged 5-6 tested productive vocabulary of French spatial prepositions (devant, sur, sous) through a quiz game on an interactive table. Children         were first taught the target words in a group French lesson, then individually assessed in either a child-robot or child-human condition using a Wizard-of-Oz controlled robot. Results show no         significant difference in production scores between robot and human conditions, indicating the robot achieved comparable performance to the human experimenter in eliciting L2 spatial vocabulary. The         robot condition showed significantly different encouragement patterns, with higher maximum encouragement levels needed in the human condition. The study supports the potential of social robots as         less intimidating assessors for productive L2 vocabulary.

title: underworlds: Cascading Situation Assessment for Robots
summary: This paper introduces Underworlds, a distributed and lightweight open-source framework for cascading spatio-temporal situation assessment in robotics. Underworlds         enables robot programmers to build and share real-time spatial and temporal models of the robot's environment as a set of interconnected 'worlds,' each containing a scene graph (3D geometries) and a         timeline (temporal events). The framework supports cascading representations where worlds can inherit from and refine each other, allowing different software components to maintain task-specific         views at varying spatial and temporal granularities. Key features include support for multiple alternative world states (e.g., filtered views, perspective-taking, hypothetical planning scenarios),         physics simulation for position correction, and seamless ROS integration. The architecture uses a client-server model with gRPC for cross-platform communication, where clients read and write to         worlds while changes cascade through dependent worlds. The paper details the design, API, standard clients for 3D visualization, physics simulation, and spatial reasoning, and discusses applications         including perspective-taking and pre-supposition accommodation.

title: What Can You See? Identifying Cues on Internal States from the Kinematics of Natural Social Interactions
venue: IDC - Workshop 'The Near Future of Children’s Robotics'
summary: This paper proposes a methodology for identifying what internal-state information humans can infer from the kinematics of naturalistic child-child social interactions. The         goal is to inform the design of artificial internal-state-reading systems for child-robot interaction. Drawing on mirror neuron system research showing that humans use observed kinematics to infer         intentions and other internal states, the authors argue that existing studies rely too heavily on artificial stimuli rather than real-world interaction data. Their proposed study uses a 2x2         between-subjects design: participants view either original video clips or skeleton-only versions (extracted via OpenPose) of children playing on a touchscreen, and answer either open-ended or         specific questions about the interaction. The skeletal condition isolates kinematic information from contextual cues. By comparing responses across conditions, the method aims to establish what         internal states (affect, cooperation, relationship quality) can be gleaned from kinematics alone, providing a baseline for what robot perception systems should be expected to detect from body         movement data.

title: Robots in the classroom: Learning to be a Good Tutor
summary: This paper presents a system for using robots as classroom tutors that learn to be effective through human supervision, applying the SPARC (Supervised Progressively Autonomous             Robot Competencies) framework to education. The authors argue that traditional robot tutoring approaches using predefined behaviors or Bayesian Knowledge Tracing are limited in handling unexpected             student behaviors. Instead, they propose that robots should learn online how to tutor by having a teacher demonstrate desired tutoring behaviors via a Wizard of Oz interface on a tablet. The robot             progressively learns from these demonstrations using a nearest-neighbor algorithm, eventually taking over the tutoring session autonomously. The system is demonstrated in a food-chain learning game             where children interact with a robot through a touchscreen, and the robot can provide advice, verbal feedback, or draw attention to game elements. The robot operates in a 210-dimensional state space             with 655 possible actions. The approach is being tested in UK primary schools with children aged 8-10, with the goal of empowering teachers to create personalized robotic tutors.

title: Social psychology and Human-Robot Interaction: an Uneasy Marriage
summary: This paper examines the relationship between social psychology and Human-Robot Interaction (HRI), highlighting the 'replication crisis' in psychology and its implications for             HRI research. The authors argue that HRI over-relies on and uncritically accepts psychological methods and findings, which is problematic given that many psychology studies fail to replicate. To             illustrate, they present their own failed attempts to replicate the well-established 'social facilitation' effect -- where the mere presence of an observer influences task performance. Across two             carefully designed studies with different tasks (shape matching, story recall, and mental arithmetic), varying observer proximity, task difficulty, and incentive structures, they found no significant             social facilitation effect from human presence on any measure including performance, cheating behavior, or perceived observation. The authors use these null results to argue that HRI should not shy             away from developing its own reference tasks and benchmarks rather than depending on borrowed psychological constructs, and should exercise greater caution when building on established social             psychology findings.

title: Qualitative Review of Object Recognition Techniques for Tabletop Manipulation
summary: This paper provides a qualitative review of object recognition techniques for near-proximity tabletop manipulation in Human-Robot Interaction contexts. The authors             evaluate techniques across three categories: 2D correspondence methods (fiducial markers, feature tracking with A-KAZE/ORB/SURF, template matching, deep learning with Faster R-CNN), 3D correspondence             methods (ORK planar segmentation, Intel Realsense SDK tracking), and non-vision-based methods (GaussSense magnetic sensors, ePawn NFC mat). Each technique is assessed on seven criteria: degrees of             freedom, detection stability, rotation invariance, distance invariance, environment interference robustness, occlusion handling, and practical use considerations. Key findings include that no single             vision-based technique fully meets tabletop manipulation requirements, though pipelines combining methods show promise. The ePawn NFC mat is identified as likely the best overall method for tabletop             object recognition due to its very high stability and invariance scores, despite lacking full 6D pose estimation. Fiducial markers are the most reliable 2D method but are sensitive to occlusion. The             paper emphasizes practical deployment considerations often missing from traditional surveys.

title: Toward Supervised Reinforcement Learning with Partial States for Social HRI
summary: This paper proposes combining the SPARC (Supervised Progressively Autonomous Robot Competencies) framework with Reinforcement Learning using partial states for social             Human-Robot Interaction. The key challenge addressed is that in social HRI, traditional RL is impractical due to high-dimensional state spaces, the impossibility of random exploration with humans,             and the absence of clear reward functions. The authors introduce 'partial states' -- sliced versions of the full state that include only dimensions highlighted by a human supervisor as relevant to a             given action. This enables generalization from very few data points by comparing only relevant features when selecting actions. The learning algorithm is instance-based: for each possible action, it             finds the closest stored partial-state/reward pair and computes an expected reward based on similarity. Combined with SPARC, the supervisor can approve, correct, or cancel robot actions while also             specifying which environmental features are relevant. The approach is illustrated in an educational scenario where a robot teaches children about food chains, and the supervisor highlights relevant             animals and their properties.

title: Supervised Autonomy for Online Learning in Human-Robot Interaction
summary: This paper presents SPARC (Supervised Progressively Autonomous Robot Competencies), a method combining interactive machine learning with supervised autonomy for online             learning in Human-Robot Interaction. SPARC gives a human teacher full control over the robot's actions through a suggestion/correction mechanism: the robot communicates its intended action, and the             teacher can approve it, correct it, or cancel it. Each approved or corrected action generates an implicit positive reward for reinforcement learning (Q-learning), eliminating the need for explicit             numerical rewards. The paper compares SPARC against Interactive Reinforcement Learning (IRL) in a user study with 40 participants using Sophie's Kitchen, a simulated environment where a virtual robot             learns to bake a cake. Three hypotheses are tested: that SPARC is more effective and efficient for non-experts, that it is safe with experts, and that teachers prefer having more control. Results             from the study evaluate training time, number of failures, teacher inputs, and subjective workload and perception measures across three repeated training sessions per method, with balanced ordering             between conditions.

title: Cellulo: Versatile Handheld Robots for Education
summary: This paper presents Cellulo, a novel robotic platform for education consisting of groups of small, low-cost, haptic-enabled handheld robots that operate on printed paper             activity sheets. The platform is designed around three goals: ubiquity (pervasive, unremarkable tools like pen and paper), versatility (applicable across many subjects, not just STEM), and             practicality (robust and easy to use in real classrooms). Each robot costs approximately 125 euros, features holonomic motion via an omnidirectional ball drive, haptic feedback, six capacitive touch             buttons, RGB backlighting, and Bluetooth connectivity to a tablet running the activity logic. Localization is achieved through a downward-facing camera reading microdot patterns printed on paper,             providing approximately 0.27mm accuracy at 93Hz without calibration. The paper presents two exploratory field studies: a Treasure Hunt with 85 children (ages 11-14) exploring passive robot use in             groups, and a Windfield activity with 145 children (ages 10-11) exploring autonomous robots for learning about wind and vectors. The studies validate the platform's technical reliability and             classroom suitability.

title: Child Speech Recognition in Human-Robot Interaction: Evaluations and Recommendations
summary: This paper evaluates automatic speech recognition (ASR) for children's speech in Human-Robot Interaction settings. The authors test multiple ASR engines under conditions             inspired by real-world child-robot interactions, collecting speech from 11 children (average age 4.9) using three microphones: the NAO robot's built-in array, a portable microphone, and a             studio-grade microphone. Key findings include: (1) audio normalization dramatically improves recognition for external microphones (from near 0% to 70-81%); (2) a studio microphone provides about 20             percentage points better recognition than the NAO's built-in microphone; (3) background noise has surprisingly little impact on recognition with constrained grammars; (4) recognition drops sharply             with distance, falling to near 0% at 100cm; and (5) for the NAO robot, recognition drops to 0% when the sound source is at 90 degrees or more from the robot's front. The paper also compares ASR             engines on open-grammar spontaneous speech, finding performance still far from adequate for reliable child-robot verbal interaction, and provides practical design recommendations for HRI             researchers.

title: Leveraging Human Inputs in Interactive Machine Learning for Human Robot Interaction
summary: This paper explores how humans use numeric rewards to evaluate a robot's behavior and guide its learning in Interactive Machine Learning (IML) for Human-Robot Interaction.             The study compares two methods: Interactive Reinforcement Learning (IRL), where users provide numerical rewards via a slider, and a command-based method (inspired by SPARC) where users directly             accept or override the robot's intended actions. In a study with 40 participants teaching a virtual robot to bake a cake, the authors find that reward distributions in the IRL condition were             tri-modal -- users primarily assigned values of -1, 0, or +1 rather than using fine-grained numerical rewards, despite having a continuous slider. From these results, the paper derives three key             challenges for designing learning robots: (1) the type of human input matters, with commands offering more control than rewards but being less generalizable; (2) the robot should clearly communicate             its internal state, intentions, uncertainty, and learning progress; and (3) the teaching process should minimize workload on human teachers while giving them sufficient control over the robot's             actions.
