title: SPARC: an efficient way to combine reinforcement learning and supervised autonomy
venue: NIPS - Proc. of the Future of Interactive Learning Machines (FILM) Workshop
summary: This paper proposes SPARC (Supervised Progressively Autonomous Robot Competencies), a method that combines reinforcement learning with supervised autonomy to teach robots             action policies. Unlike Interactive Reinforcement Learning (IRL), where users provide rewards and guidance, SPARC uses a suggestion/correction mechanism where the robot proposes actions and a human             supervisor can either accept or override them. The supervisor's corrections are converted into positive rewards for reinforcement learning. A study with 40 participants using a virtual kitchen task             ('Sophie's kitchen') compared SPARC to IRL across four metrics. SPARC significantly outperformed IRL: median task performance was 6 vs. 0, teaching time was shorter, the number of failures was lower             (3 vs. 9), and fewer inputs were required (141 vs. 248). The results demonstrate that SPARC is a more efficient method for non-expert users to interactively teach robots, as it avoids the challenges             of reward interpretation inherent in feedback-based approaches while keeping the human in full control during learning.

title: Towards Machine-Learnable Child-Robot Interactions: the PInSoRo Dataset
venue: RoMAN - Workshop on Long-term Child-robot Interaction
summary: This paper proposes the PInSoRo (Plymouth Interacting Social Robots) dataset, a large open dataset of child-robot social interactions intended to advance machine learning             for social HRI. The authors argue that deep learning has potential for generating social robot behaviours but lacks suitable training data. The proposed methodology involves children aged 8-14             interacting with a Nao robot puppeted by an expert adult across four face-to-face social tasks: spatial reasoning, collaborative storytelling, a Sokoban-inspired collaborative strategy game, and a             Taboo party game. These tasks are designed to elicit rich multimodal social behaviours including speech, gesture, and gaze. The dataset would record synchronized robot joint states, RGB+depth video             from multiple perspectives, and audio. The authors discuss potential applications including probabilistic reactive behaviour models and training deep neural networks for social behaviour generation.             They also address methodological concerns such as the visibility of the puppeteer and the constraints of the robot embodiment on natural interaction.

title: The Cautious Attitude of Teachers Towards Social Robots in Schools
venue: RoMAN - Workshop on Robots for Learning
summary: This paper investigates attitudes of teachers and the general public towards the use of social robots in schools. A survey was administered to 87 members of the general             public (via crowdsourcing) and 35 education professionals from two UK primary schools. Two versions of the survey included images of a classroom with or without a human teacher present, though this             manipulation produced no significant differences. Key findings include: educators are more cautious than the general public about robots in education; teachers predominantly see robots as tools for             STEM subjects rather than educative agents; the most cited obstacle among educators is the robot being a source of distraction (34.5%), followed by a perceived lack of social skills (31%); and             concerns about child isolation were unexpectedly raised. Differences between schools suggest micro-cultural factors influence acceptance. The general public was more concerned about practical issues             like cost. Notably, very few ethical or safety concerns were raised. The findings suggest that appropriate social skills for robots should be a priority for child-robot interaction research.

title: Artificial Cognition for Social Human-Robot Interaction: An Implementation
bibtex:
@article{lemaignan2017artificial,
journal = {Artificial Intelligence},
title = {Artificial Cognition for Social Human-Robot Interaction: An Implementation},
publisher = {Elsevier},
year = {2017},
author = {Lemaignan, S. and Warnier, M. and Sisbot, E. A. and  Clodic, A. and Alami, R.},
doi = {10.1016/j.artint.2016.07.002}}
summary: This article presents a comprehensive deliberative architecture for social human-robot interaction, focusing on the cognitive capabilities needed for robots to share             space and collaborate with humans. The architecture is built around explicit knowledge representation using RDF/OWL ontologies managed by a central knowledge base (ORO server). It integrates six main             components: geometric and temporal situation assessment (SPARK), symbolic knowledge management (ORO), natural language processing (DIALOGS), human-aware symbolic task planning (HATP), human-aware             motion and manipulation planning (MHP), and execution control (SHARY/pyRobots). The key design principle is that human-level interaction requires the robot to internally rely on human-level             semantics, achieved through first-order logic statements organized into ontologies. The architecture supports perspective-taking, affordance analysis, multi-agent knowledge models, situated dialogue,             and joint task achievement. It is demonstrated on multiple robotic platforms and distinguishes itself from cognitive architectures by focusing on effective artificial cognition rather than             replicating human cognition.

title: Children's Peer Assessment and Self-disclosure in the Presence of an Educational Robot
summary: This paper examines how children assess their peers and disclose personal information to a robot facilitator during collaborative writing activities. Forty             Portuguese-speaking children (ages 6-8) participated in pairs, using tablets with a Nao robot facilitator in two conditions: peer-tutoring (PT), where one child is assigned as teacher and the other             as learner, and peer-learning (PL), where no roles are assigned. Results show that in the PT condition, learner-children provided significantly more extended corrective feedback to their peers             compared to children in the PL condition. Similarly, self-disclosure to the robot was significantly higher in the PT condition for both tutor and learner children. Furthermore, the PT condition             showed significant learning gains (pre- to post-test improvement), while the PL condition did not. The findings suggest that assigning explicit roles in robot-mediated peer learning influences both             assessment behaviour and self-disclosure, with the peer-tutoring method producing more substantive feedback and greater engagement with the robot facilitator.

title: Learning by Teaching a Robot: The Case of Handwriting
summary: This paper presents a 'learning by teaching' approach to handwriting remediation for children with graphomotor difficulties. A Nao robot acts as a poor writer that             children teach to improve, leveraging the 'protege effect' where teaching reinforces the teacher's own learning and self-esteem. The system uses PCA-based shape models built from adult handwriting             datasets to both generate plausible bad letters and learn from children's demonstrations. Letters are represented in eigenspace rather than Cartesian space, enabling semantically meaningful             distortions and progressive improvement. The robot performs simulated handwriting by gesturing in the air while a synchronized tablet displays the letters. Children correct the robot's attempts by             overwriting on the tablet, and the robot learns from these demonstrations. The paper reports on multiple field experiments including four school studies, a clinical study with eight children at an             occupational therapy clinic, and two month-long case studies with children with actual handwriting deficits. The approach addresses technical feasibility, acceptance by practitioners, sustained             engagement over time, and initial evidence of remediation efficacy.

title: The Symmetry of Partner Modelling
venue: Intl. J. of Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning
bibtex:
@article{dillenbourg2016symmetry,
author = {Pierre Dillenbourg and Séverin Lemaignan and Mirweis Sangin and Nicolas Nova and Gaëlle Molinari},
title = {The Symmetry of Partner Modelling},
journal = {Intl. J. of Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning},
publisher = {Springer},
issn= {1556-1615},
doi= {10.1007/s11412-016-9235-5},
year = {2016}}
summary: This paper investigates the symmetry of partner modelling in collaborative learning contexts. Partner modelling refers to the process of inferring a collaborator's             mental states, knowledge, skills, and intentions during joint work. Across five experiments on collaborative problem solving and learning, the authors examine whether the accuracy of the model that             person A builds of person B correlates with the accuracy of B's model of A. In four of the five studies, this correlation was found, suggesting that the quality of interactions matters more than             individual abilities for building accurate mutual models. The paper grounds this investigation in the CSCL (Computer Supported Collaborative Learning) literature on shared understanding and             grounding, discussing the tension between shallow linguistic alignment and deep semantic grounding. The authors define partner modelling as ranging from dispositional aspects (long-term knowledge                 about a partner) to situational aspects (context-specific representations during collaboration), and discuss how models are initialized through stereotypes and co-presence, then updated through                 verbal and non-verbal interaction cues.

title: Real-Time High-Accuracy 2D Localization with Structured Patterns
summary: This paper presents a novel 2D localization technique for mobile robots based on structured printed dot patterns, adapted from the Anoto positioning technology                 originally designed for digital pens. The method uses quasi De Bruijn sequences to encode unique positions via micro-dot patterns organized in a grid. The localization pipeline processes captured                 images through blob detection, grid estimation using a constrained k-means algorithm, likelihood assignment for symbol classification, best region selection, orientation correction, and decoding. The                 system achieves sub-millimeter absolute positioning accuracy (below 0.3mm) with low cost (below 30 euros per device at prototype stage), high computational efficiency (above 45Hz on a low-cost                     microcontroller), and unlimited scalability. The reference hardware implementation uses a single PCB with off-the-shelf components including NIR LEDs, a low-cost image sensor, and a PIC32                     microcontroller. The technique offers a unique combination of low cost, high accuracy, and full occlusion robustness, making it suitable for indoor mobile robotics applications including swarm                     robotics and human-robot interaction scenarios.

title: Providing a Robot with Learning Abilities Improves its Perception by Users
summary: This paper examines how providing a robot with learning capabilities affects users' subjective perception and preferences when supervising the robot. Using the SPARC                     framework, ten participants each supervised two wizarded-robots in a child-robot interaction scenario involving a face classification task: one with random actions (non-learning) and one using                     reinforcement learning via a Multi-Layer Perceptron. Overall, 6 out of 10 participants preferred supervising the learning robot and 8 out of 10 rated it as better at performing the task. The study                     also revealed an ordering effect, where the second robot interacted with tended to be preferred regardless of condition. The results suggest that equipping robots with learning abilities improves                     user perception even when objective task performance may be lower, indicating that learning capabilities could reduce the apparent workload for supervisors and facilitate integration of robots into                     workflows such as robot-assisted therapy.

title: Workshop on Cognitive Architectures for Social Human-Robot Interaction
summary: This paper is a workshop proposal for the second Workshop on Cognitive Architectures for Social Human-Robot Interaction, held at the 2016 HRI conference. It argues that                     social HRI requires robots capable of appropriate, adaptive, and contingent behaviours, and that Cognitive Architectures provide a principled framework for achieving this by proposing general                     mechanisms of intelligence and behaviour generation. The workshop focuses specifically on cognitive architectures for social interaction, building on the first edition held in 2014. It invites                     position papers addressing six guiding questions covering topics such as whether cognitive architectures should be inspired by human cognition, basic requirements for social interaction,                     computational structure choices (symbolic, sub-symbolic, hybrid), outstanding challenges, and failure scenarios for current architectures. The workshop aims to bring together researchers from diverse                     backgrounds to facilitate discussion and establish common ground for comparing different approaches to cognitive robot control for social interaction.

title: From Characterising Three Years of HRI to Methodology and Reporting Recommendations
bibtex:
@inproceedings{baxter2016characterising,
author = {Baxter, P. and Kennedy, J. and Senft E. and Lemaignan, S. and Belpaeme, T.},
title = {From Characterising Three Years of HRI to Methodology and Reporting Recommendations},
booktitle = {Proceedings of the 2016 ACM/IEEE Human-Robot Interaction Conference (alt.HRI)},
isbn = {978-1-4673-8370-7},
doi = {10.1109/HRI.2016.7451777},
year = {2016}}
summary: This paper characterises three years of publications (2013-2015) from the ACM/IEEE HRI conference, analysing over 100 full papers across 14 categories related to methodology and reporting. The authors identify six broad challenges in HRI research: robot autonomy, participant populations, evaluation environments, study length, statistical approaches, and replicability. Key findings include that only 40% of studies used fully autonomous robots, 87% of non-child studies relied on university convenience samples, 75% were lab-based, and 95% used Null-Hypothesis Significance Testing. The paper provides practical recommendations for improving methodological rigor, including clearer reporting of Wizard-of-Oz usage, more representative participant samples, greater use of field studies, longer-term investigations to address novelty effects, and supplementing p-values with effect sizes and descriptive statistics. The authors advocate for a common methodological framework that preserves the interdisciplinary nature of HRI while facilitating rigorous, comparable research.

title: From Real-time Attention Assessment to “With-me-ness” in Human-Robot Interaction
award: HRI'16 Best 'Theory and Methods' Paper Award
summary: This paper presents a methodology for measuring 'with-me-ness' in human-robot interaction, a concept borrowed from Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning that captures the extent to which a human is attentionally 'with' the robot during an interactive task. The approach combines real-time estimation of the human's visual focus of attention, derived from 6D head pose estimation using a monocular RGB camera and the dlib face alignment library, with knowledge of expected attentional targets for the current task. The system approximates the field of attention as a 40-degree cone from the face and determines which objects of interest fall within it. Validated in a field study with six children (ages 5-6) during a handwriting remediation task with a NAO robot, the system tracked head poses 86% of the time on average, with moderate-to-substantial agreement (Cohen's kappa 0.26-0.68) against manual video annotations. The with-me-ness metric provides an in-the-moment, online measure of interaction quality without requiring post-hoc video coding.

title: Building Successful Long Child-Robot Interactions in a Learning Context
summary: This paper reports on the CoWriter project, which uses a 'learning by teaching' paradigm to help children with handwriting difficulties. A NAO robot pretends to need help learning to write, and the child acts as the teacher, thereby leveraging the 'protege effect' to boost the child's self-confidence and motivation. The paper presents refined algorithms for generating initial letter shapes and learning from children's demonstrations using PCA-based subspaces, and describes three case studies involving children with real handwriting difficulties in long-term interventions (four sessions of 40-60 minutes each). In the first case study, a 5-year-old with low self-confidence maintained engagement across four weekly sessions and showed improved motivation and handwriting. The second involved a 5.5-year-old with visuo-constructive deficits, working with his therapist. The system is implemented on an Aldebaran NAO robot using ROS, with tactile tablets for the writing interaction. Results suggest the approach can sustain children's commitment over extended periods.

title: The Wider Supportive Role of Social Robots in the Classroom for Teachers
summary: This paper examines the broader implications of deploying social robots in school classrooms from the teacher's perspective, based on qualitative observations from 15 school visits to 9 different UK schools over three years. The authors identify several often-overlooked factors: teachers consistently adapted their behavior around the robot, using it as a motivational tool or 'bribe' to manage classroom behavior, which inadvertently primes children's expectations and introduces methodological confounds. A longer-term embedded study where robots were placed in classrooms for two weeks without experimenters revealed that while the novelty-driven distraction wore off in the second week, the robot's motivational value persisted. The paper derives three themes for future research: pedagogy (how the robot fits the teacher's style and curriculum), methodology (accounting for teacher-induced expectation effects on experimental conditions), and ethics (how the teacher's treatment of the robot shapes children's perceptions and trust).

title: Ranger, an Example of Integration of Robotics into the Home Ecosystem
venue: New Trends in Medical and Service Robots
bibtex:
@inbook{mondada2015ranger,
author = {Mondada, F. and Fink, J. and Lemaignan, S. and Mansolino, D. and Wille, F. and Franinović, K.},
chapter = {Ranger, an Example of Integration of Robotics into the Home Ecosystem},
title = {New Trends in Medical and Service Robots},
publisher = {Springer Publishing},
series = {Mechan. Machine Science},
volume = {38},
year = {2015},
doi = {10.1007/978-3-319-23832-6_15},
isbn = {978-3-319-23831-9},
note = {Appeared first as a paper at MESROB2014}}
summary: This paper introduces the concept of 'robjects' -- robotic entities embedded in everyday objects -- and presents the 'ranger' robot as an example. The ranger is built from a children's wooden toy storage box augmented with wheels, mechanical eyes, sensors (distance, ground, inertial, capacitive touch), LED panels, sound, a detachable pacifier, and a range-and-bearing system. Designed by an interdisciplinary team of engineers, designers, and ethnographers, the ranger aims to encourage children to tidy their rooms by leveraging the familiar affordance of a storage box. The robot uses four processors (a Linux board and three microcontrollers connected via CAN bus), with ASEBA and ROS for control, SLAM, and navigation. The paper advocates a holistic approach to domestic robotics that balances functionality, cost, and integration into the home ecosystem, arguing this improves acceptance compared to traditional humanoid service robots. User tests in families validated the approach and demonstrated the impact of ecosystem-aware design.

title: You're Doing It Wrong! Studying Unexpected Behaviors in Child-Robot Interaction
summary: This paper studies the impact of unexpected robot behaviors on children's engagement and anthropomorphic perceptions during a collaborative domino game. Twenty-six children aged 4-5 played in pairs with the Ranger robot, which transported domino tiles between them. Three types of 'misbehavior' were tested in a between-subjects design: getting lost (system error), disobeying (showing intentionality by refusing and shaking its head), and making a mistake (going wrong then self-correcting). The authors propose a novel compound index blending behavioral cues (touch, talk, gestures, misuse) with interview-based phenomenological perceptions to assess anthropomorphism. Key findings include sustained engagement levels across all conditions, limited recognition of the different misbehavior types (attributed to the young age of participants), unexpectedly low attribution of higher cognitive abilities to the robot, and a negative correlation between anthropomorphic perceptions and actual behavioral engagement -- suggesting that children who anthropomorphize the robot more do not necessarily engage more actively with it.

title: pyRobots: a Toolset for Robot Executive Control
summary: This paper presents pyRobots, an open-source Python toolkit for high-level executive control of robotic systems. Designed bottom-up from practical needs in complex human-robot interaction scenarios, pyRobots offers a lightweight, unobtrusive framework for writing parallel, event-based robot controllers. Key features include asynchronous actions implemented as futures with support for cancellation via signaling threads, an event monitoring system with predicate-based conditions, a resource locking mechanism for exclusive access to physical components (arms, wheels, etc.), uniform management of 6D poses and rigid transformations across different reference frames, and logging/debugging tools with offline replay. The toolkit is middleware-agnostic but integrates with ROS. Deployments are reported across four projects: a theatre performance with a PR2 robot, interactive grounding experiments, the CoWriter handwriting project with NAO, and a stress-test with Ranger robots in a kindergarten involving 17 children, where the system sustained an average of 70 actions per minute over 68 minutes.

title: A Review: Can Robots Reshape K-12 STEM Education?
summary: This review surveys existing literature on robot-based learning activities for K-12 mathematics and physics education, deliberately excluding 'robots to teach robotics.' The paper provides an updated definition of educational robotics aligned with constructionism, learning by design, active learning, and social constructivism. It catalogs mathematics topics (geometric primitives, counting, fractions, coordinate systems, graphs) and physics topics (kinematics, forces, Doppler effect, electricity) that have been taught using robots. A comprehensive comparison of robotic platforms is presented across six categories: brick-based kits (LEGO Mindstorms, VEX IQ), minimal mobile robot kits, robot manipulators, open-source designs, pre-assembled desktop robots (including Thymio and e-puck), and miniaturized swarm robots. The authors also review augmented reality approaches for STEM education. Key shortcomings identified include limited presence of robots in formal curricula, inadequate teacher training, and the lack of an educational framework merging robot tangibility with augmented reality.

title: Can a Child Feel Responsible for Another in the Presence of a Robot in a Collaborative Learning Activity?
summary: This paper investigates whether a robot facilitator affects children's sense of responsibility toward a peer in a collaborative learning activity. Forty Portuguese-speaking children aged 6-8 participated in pairs in a learning-by-teaching handwriting task on tactile tablets, with either a NAO robot or a human acting as the facilitator. Using a Wizard-of-Oz setup, the study measured corrective feedback (verbal behavior), gaze patterns, and interpersonal distancing. Results show that teacher-children provided more extended corrective feedback to learner-children in the robot facilitator condition, suggesting greater felt responsibility. The interaction patterns followed different interpersonal distancing models: the robot condition exhibited a reciprocity model (mutual approach), while the human condition followed a compensation model (balanced withdrawal and approach). Learning gains were significant in both conditions with no significant difference between them, supporting the hypothesis that a robot facilitator can be as effective as a human for supporting collaborative learning while eliciting different social dynamics.

title: Mutual Modelling in Robotics: Inspirations for the Next Steps
summary: This paper provides a broad interdisciplinary overview of mutual modelling -- the reciprocal ability to build mental models of others -- and its relevance to social robotics. Drawing from three academic domains, it first reviews developmental psychology, covering theory of mind (ToM), the distinction between cognitive connections and representations in Flavell's perspective-taking levels, tasks from autism research (false belief, deception, information occlusion), and higher-order ToM. It then examines psycholinguistics and Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning (CSCL), discussing grounding, shared understanding, and Dillenbourg's model of mutual modelling with its notions of accuracy, symmetry, and optimal collaborative effort. Finally, it introduces formal epistemology and epistemic logic as tools for reasoning about knowledge in group interactions. The paper argues that robotics, as the science of embodied AI, can serve as a convergence point for validating theories of social cognition, and identifies key open questions about representing, accessing, and comparing mutual models in human-robot interaction scenarios.

title: When Children Teach a Robot to Write: An Autonomous Teachable Humanoid Which Uses Simulated Handwriting
summary: This paper presents a novel robotic system where children teach a NAO humanoid robot to write, leveraging the 'learning by teaching' paradigm to stimulate meta-cognition,                                                                                 empathy, and self-esteem. The robot uses simulated handwriting on a synchronized tablet display, with statistical shape models derived from PCA of adult-written letter trajectories enabling it to                                                                                 generate purposefully deformed letters. The robot learns from children's demonstrations by updating its shape model parameters. Preliminary in-situ studies with over 50 children aged 6-8 at primary                                                                                 schools showed that children successfully engaged with the robot, believed it was learning, and improved its writing to satisfactory levels. The system integrates a NAO robot, an Android tablet, and                                                                                 a central controller communicating via ROS, with the robot pointing at a vertical tablet surface rather than using a pen. The work addresses a socially meaningful challenge in education, providing an                                                                                 innovative use of robotics for handwriting intervention.

title: The CoWriter Project: Teaching a Robot how to Write
summary: This is a video companion paper for the CoWriter project, accompanying the full 'When Children Teach a Robot to Write' paper. It presents the first results of the EPFL                                                                                 CoWriter project, which builds a robotic partner that children can teach handwriting. The system uses the learning by teaching paradigm with a NAO humanoid robot and a synchronized tablet display.                                                                                 Statistical shape models from PCA allow the robot to draw deformed letters, and user demonstrations enable learning. Preliminary studies with about 50 children aged 6-8 in the Geneva area validated                                                                                 the system: children engaged with the robot and improved its writing. The video demonstrates how the robot simulates handwriting, generates poor letters, and responds to children's demonstrations.                                                                                 The source code is available on GitHub.

title: A Few AI Challenges Raised while Developing an Architecture for Human-Robot Cooperative Task Achievement
venue: AAAI 2014 Fall Symposium Series - Artificial Intelligence and Human-Robot Interaction
summary: This paper identifies key AI challenges encountered while developing an architecture for autonomous human-robot cooperative task achievement over five years. The                                                                                 authors present a robotic architecture centered on explicit knowledge management using the ORO knowledge base as a semantic blackboard. The architecture includes SPARK for geometric reasoning,                                                                                 DIALOGS for language processing, HATP for human-aware task planning, and an execution controller. The paper defines 'cognitive skills' as deliberative behaviors that are stateful, amodal, manipulate                                                                                 explicit grounded semantics, and operate at human level. Four key capabilities are discussed: symbolic reasoning and mutual modelling (including theory of mind via perspective taking), natural                                                                                 language grounding (establishing common ground between human and robot), and human-aware task planning using the HATP planner that generates interleaved shared plans with social rules for workload                                                                                 balancing. The overarching message is that explicit knowledge management, both symbolic and geometric, is key to addressing decision-making in human-robot interaction.

title: Which Robot Behavior Can Motivate Children to Tidy up Their Toys? Design and Evaluation of “Ranger”
summary: This paper presents the design and evaluation of 'Ranger,' a robotic toy box aimed at motivating young children to tidy up their rooms. Ranger is a wheeled box with eyes,                                                                                 eyebrows, LEDs, and speakers that provides feedback when children interact with it. The study evaluated Ranger with 31 children (aged 2-10) across 14 families using a Wizard-of-Oz methodology,                                                                                 comparing two robot behaviors: proactive (driving around searching for toys) and reactive (staying in place and responding to actions). Video analysis of tidying scenarios revealed that the proactive                                                                                 robot encouraged more playful and explorative behavior, while the reactive robot triggered more actual tidying behavior. Children naturally understood how to use Ranger regardless of behavior mode,                                                                                 starting to put toys in the box within about two minutes. Parents rated Ranger positively on ease of use and enjoyment. The findings offer implications for designing interactive domestic robots for                                                                                 children, particularly regarding the balance between proactive and reactive behaviors.

title: The Dynamics of Anthropomorphism in Robotics
summary: This short paper proposes a formal model of the dynamics of anthropomorphism in human-robot interaction. Unlike the traditional view of anthropomorphism as a static                                                                                 feature, the authors argue it is a dynamic, non-monotonic, and context-dependent process evolving over time during long-term interactions. The model identifies three main phases: initialization (a                                                                                     short phase where anthropomorphism increases from an initial capital of anthropomorphism to a peak due to the novelty effect), familiarization (lasting days, where anthropomorphism decreases as users                                                                                     learn to predict the robot's behavior), and stabilization (reaching a stabilized level of anthropomorphism). Disruptive or unexpected robot behaviors can cause local increases in anthropomorphism                                                                                     during later phases. The model also identifies three cognitive phases: pre-cognitive identification of living peers driven by empathy, projection of familiar mental models onto the robot, and                                                                                     iterative adaptation of mental models during contextualized interaction. The work aims to consolidate the scientific grounds of anthropomorphism for long-term robot acceptance.

title: Simulation and HRI - Recent Perspectives with the MORSE Simulator
bibtex:
@inproceedings{lemaignan2014simulation,
author = {Lemaignan, S. and Hanheide, M. and Karg, M. and Khambhaita, H. and Kunze, L. and Lier, F. and Lütkebohle, I. and Milliez, G.},
title = {Simulation and HRI -- Recent Perspectives with the MORSE Simulator},
booktitle = {Proceedings of the International Conference on Simulation, Modeling, and Programming for Autonomous Robots (SIMPAR)},
year = {2014},
publisher = {Springer},
series = {LNAI Lecture Notes in Artificial Intelligence}}
summary: This collective article reports on five independent applications of the MORSE simulator for human-robot interaction research. MORSE is an open-source simulator built                                                                                     on the Blender Game Engine that supports multiple middleware bindings (ROS, YARP) and provides a controllable human avatar for HRI scenarios. The paper categorizes simulation use-cases into                                                                                     prototyping, human modeling, interactive simulation, and anticipatory simulation. The five case studies include: situation assessment for HRI using SPARK with MORSE providing semantic cameras; an                                                                                     expectations framework for domestic robot assistants detecting unexpected human behavior; testing a human-aware navigation planner; data acquisition through automatic scene generation; and automated                                                                                     execution of prototype HRI experiments integrated into software development workflows. The paper argues that simulation is already useful and sometimes essential for HRI research, despite the                                                                                     inherent challenge of modeling unpredictable humans, and that MORSE's domain-independent design and abstraction levels make it particularly suitable for this purpose.

title: The Cognitive Correlates of Anthropomorphism
venue: Workshop A bridge between Robotics and Neuroscience - HRI
summary: This paper extends a previously proposed model of the dynamics of anthropomorphism by investigating its cognitive correlates. The authors propose three cognitive phases                                                                                     that parallel the phenomenological phases of anthropomorphism. Phase I is sub-cognitive, instinctive identification of living peers, characterized by empathy and potentially mediated by mirror                                                                                     neuron systems. Phase II involves the projection of existing familiar mental models onto the robot after observation, drawing on the familiarity thesis that we understand the world through mental                                                                                     models of what we know best. Phase III occurs during contextualized interaction where the human iteratively reshapes their cognitive model of the robot based on experience. The paper discusses two                                                                                     perspectives on anthropomorphism: artifact-centered (responding to lifelike cues) and human-centered (constructing mental models). It notes that initial capital of anthropomorphism and stabilized                                                                                     level of anthropomorphism are generally not correlated, and raises questions about whether robots could leverage these cognitive models to manage long-term interactions more effectively.

title: Explicit Knowledge and the Deliberative Layer: Lessons Learned
summary: This paper presents lessons learned from building a knowledge-oriented architecture for autonomous robots at LAAS-CNRS. Over four years, the authors integrated explicit knowledge representation and manipulation into the deliberative and executive layers of their robots, centered around the OpenRobot Ontology (ORO) server acting as a semantic blackboard. The architecture uses RDF triples and OWL ontologies with Description Logics reasoning. Key components include SPARK for geometric and temporal situation assessment (computing spatial relations, visibility, and reachability from agent-dependent perspectives), the Dialogs natural language grounding module, the HATP hierarchical task planner for human-aware planning, and event-driven execution control. The system supports theory of mind through separate knowledge models per agent, enabling false-belief reasoning. The authors discuss strengths and weaknesses of explicit knowledge management, arguing it pushes toward a more semantic approach to robot decision-making.

title: Grounding the Interaction: Knowledge Management for Interactive Robots [dissertation abstract]
bibtex:
@article{lemaignan2013grounding,
author={Lemaignan, Séverin},
title={Grounding the Interaction: Knowledge Management for Interactive Robots},
year={2013},
issn={0933-1875},
journal={KI - Künstliche Intelligenz},
volume={27},
number={2},
doi={10.1007/s13218-013-0246-3},
publisher={Springer-Verlag},
pages={183-185}}
summary: This dissertation abstract summarizes Lemaignan's PhD thesis on knowledge management for interactive robots. The thesis addresses three challenges: clarifying what knowledge representation means for robotics, building a knowledge-enabled robot, and applying it to human-robot interaction. The first part proposes a typology of over thirty desirable features for knowledge representation systems in service robotics, organized into six categories (expressiveness, representation format, reasoning techniques, knowledge acquisition, availability to other components, and knowledge instantiation). The second part presents the OpenRobots Ontology (ORO) framework, a semantic blackboard where robotic modules read and write RDF triples, supporting multiple mental models for theory of mind, concept discrimination, and memory management. The third part focuses on situated natural language dialogue grounding. The thesis bridges research on disembodied cognitive architectures with the constraints of real-world robotic systems.

title: Talking to my Robot: from Knowledge Grounding to Dialogue Processing
summary: This video paper presents tools developed at LAAS-CNRS for symbol grounding and natural language processing for companion robots. It focuses on two main components: the ORO-server knowledge base and the Dialogs natural language processor. Together, these enable three cognitive functions for natural human-robot interaction: a theory of mind built upon perspective taking, multi-modal communication combining verbal input with gestures, and symbol grounding with disambiguation supported by the first two abilities. The robot builds a geometric model online using Kinect-like sensors, performs situation assessment to generate perspective-dependent symbolic models stored as ontologies, and grounds verbal input by parsing and semantically interpreting user utterances against this symbolic model. The overall architecture integrates with a 3D motion planner, an HTN symbolic task planner, and an execution controller.

title: Natural Interaction for Object Hand-Over
summary: This video paper demonstrates several abilities and algorithms for interactive 'pick and give' tasks between a robot and a human in a domestic environment. The system aims for natural and legible robot behavior: natural because the human communicates via unconstrained spoken English mixed with gestures and perspective taking, and legible because human-aware motion planning algorithms account for implicit social rules in placement and approach strategies. The architecture includes geometric modeling of the environment, real-time symbolic model construction with affordances like visibility and reachability, natural language grounding via the Dialogs module, human-aware motion planning respecting proxemics constraints, and a planner that shares effort in handover tasks by proactively choosing optimal handover configurations for both seated and standing humans, using a notion of mobility to tune the effort asked of the human.

title: Grounding the Interaction: Knowledge Management for Interactive Robots
venue: CNRS - LAAS | TU München - Intelligent Autonomous Systems lab
summary: This PhD thesis by Severin Lemaignan, jointly awarded by Universite Paul Sabatier (Toulouse) and Technische Universitat Munchen, addresses knowledge management for interactive robots. The thesis is structured in three main parts: (1) a systematic study of symbolic knowledge representation requirements for service robotics and human-robot interaction, proposing a typology of desirable features and reviewing nine existing knowledge representation systems; (2) the design, development, and integration of the OpenRobots Ontology (ORO) framework, a centralized semantic blackboard using RDF/OWL that supports multiple agent models, theory of mind, concept discrimination, and bio-inspired memory management; and (3) knowledge-enabled situated natural language processing, covering dialogue grounding algorithms and experiments. The thesis was tested on multiple robot platforms and concludes by advocating for knowledge-oriented architectures where explicit knowledge flows are made tangible and queryable across robot subsystems.

title: Simulating complex robotic scenarios with MORSE
summary: This paper presents the state of MORSE (Modular Open Robots Simulation Engine), a robotics simulation framework built on the Blender 3D game engine. MORSE is designed to test and evaluate integrated robot software rather than focusing on a single robotics domain. It provides realistic rendering and physics simulation via Blender and the Bullet physics library, supports multiple levels of abstraction, and offers software-in-the-loop testing with middleware-agnostic components connectable to ROS, Pocolibs, YARP, and others. Key features include a component library of configurable sensors, actuators, and robots, a Python-based Builder API for scene construction, modifiers for adjusting sensor realism, and support for distributed multi-robot simulations. MORSE is open-source (BSD license), cross-platform, and designed to adapt to existing robotic architectures without requiring modifications to the robot software being tested.

title: Towards a Platform-Independent Cooperative Human-Robot Interaction System: III. An Architecture for Learning and Executing Actions and Shared Plans
venue: IEEE Transactions on Autonomous Mental Development
bibtex:
