author = {Stéphane Lallée and Ugo Pattacini and Séverin Lemaignan and Alexander
  Lenz and Chris Melhuish and Lorenzo Natale and Sergey Skachek and
  Katharina Hamann and Jasmin Steinwender and Emrah Akin Sisbot and
  Giorgio Metta and Tony Pipe and Rachid Alami and Matthieu Warnier
   and Julien Guitton and Felix Warneken and Peter Ford Dominey},
title = {Towards a Platform-Independent Cooperative Human Robot Interaction
  System: III. An Architecture for Learning and Executing Actions and
  Shared Plans},
journal = {IEEE Transactions on Autonomous Mental Development},
year = {2012}}

title: When the Robot Puts Itself in Your Shoes. Managing and Exploiting Human and Robot Beliefs
summary: This paper presents new spatio-temporal reasoning skills for a cognitive robot that explicitly reasons about human beliefs regarding object positions. The robot builds independent symbolic models reflecting each agent's perspective on the world, enabling it to detect, update, and delete position-related beliefs in real time. The authors introduce an algorithm that tracks three variables per agent per object (positionKnown, hasDistinctPosition, distinctPosition) to handle scenarios where a human leaves and returns to find objects moved. Two key scenarios are demonstrated: false/distinct belief (the human thinks an object is somewhere it is not) and lack of belief (the human does not know where an object is). The system uses SPARK for geometric reasoning, ORO for symbolic knowledge storage, and HATP for human-aware task planning. Experiments show the robot can answer questions and proactively inform humans based on belief divergence.

title: Roboscopie: A Theatre Performance for a Human and a Robot
summary: This video abstract describes Roboscopie, an 18-minute live theatre performance for a general public audience (over 300 people) featuring professional actor Xavier Brossard and the LAAS/CNRS PR2 robot. Created and directed by mixed-media artist Nicolas Darrot, the play was programmed over two months using the LAAS cognitive architecture, including the SPARK situation assessment module, the ORO ontology-based knowledge base, and the Dialogs natural language processor. The storyline explores how humans and robots find common ground in a frontier world of abstract, disembodied objects represented by 2D barcodes. The significance for HRI research is twofold: it demonstrated research-grade cognitive software running reliably in front of a public audience, and the narrative highlighted key questions about human-robot coexistence, mutual understanding, and power dynamics.

title: Human-Robot Interaction in the MORSE Simulator
summary: This late-breaking report describes the evolution of the MORSE simulator into a full-fledged human-robot interaction simulation environment. Built on the Blender Game Engine with Bullet physics, MORSE uses a Software In The Loop architecture where simulated sensors and actuators exchange data with real robotics software via middleware bindings (ROS, YARP, etc.). Key HRI additions include a human avatar controllable in first-person via keyboard, WiiMote, or Kinect, enabling immersive interaction with the simulated environment (picking objects, opening drawers). A semantic camera sensor exports abstracted object information (name, type, color, location) to simplify HRI experiments. The simulator is integrated with the LAAS cognitive architecture, including SPARK for geometric reasoning, and is used at both LAAS-CNRS Toulouse and Munich TU for evaluating human-aware motion planning and natural language interaction scenarios.

title: When the robot considers the human...
summary: This paper presents a comprehensive robot control system designed for cognitive robots that share space and collaborate with humans. The authors propose a decisional framework                                                                                                                                         built on three core activities: situation assessment and context management, goals and plans management, and action refinement/execution/monitoring. Key components include SPARK for geometric and                                                                                                                                         temporal reasoning, ORO for ontology-based knowledge management, and HATP for human-aware task planning. The system uses perspective-taking to maintain separate cognitive models for each agent (human                                                                                                                                             and robot), enabling the robot to reason about what the human can see, reach, and know. The architecture supports human-aware motion and manipulation planning, allowing the robot to produce socially                                                                                                                                             acceptable behaviors. The framework is demonstrated on a real robot performing face-to-face interactive tasks with a human partner in domestic environments.

title: Grounding the Interaction: Anchoring Situated Discourse in Everyday Human-Robot Interaction
bibtex:
@article{lemaignan2011grounding,
author = {S'everin Lemaignan and Raquel Ros and E. Akin Sisbot and Rachid Alami and Michael Beetz},
title = {Grounding the Interaction: Anchoring Situated Discourse in Everyday Human-Robot Interaction},
journal = {International Journal of Social Robotics},
publisher = {Springer Netherlands},
issn = {1875-4791},
pages = {1-19},
year = {2011},
url = {http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s12369-011-0123-x}}
summary: This paper presents an integrated approach to grounding human-robot interaction in symbolic knowledge. It addresses how extraction, representation, and use of symbolic                                                                                                                                             knowledge from perception and verbal/non-verbal interaction can create a shared model of the world suitable for dialogue understanding. Three inter-related cognitive components are developed: (1)                                                                                                                                             physical environment modeling and spatial reasoning (SPARK) that builds a coherent 3D model and computes symbolic beliefs from geometric data including perspective-taking; (2) a knowledge                                                                                                                                             representation system (ORO) based on Description Logics that maintains separate belief models for each agent; and (3) dialogue processing that handles statements, orders, and questions through                                                                                                                                             natural language parsing and concept anchoring. The paper introduces the concept of a knowledge-oriented architecture where all components communicate via streams of symbolic facts. Contributions                                                                                                                                             include a versatile ontology-based knowledge base, a framework for extracting symbolic facts from complex scenes, and situated natural language grounding capabilities, validated on multiple robotic                                                                                                                                             platforms.

title: Towards a Platform-Independent Cooperative Human-Robot Interaction System: II. Perception, Execution and Imitation of Goal Directed Actions
summary: This paper presents a platform-independent cooperative human-robot interaction system focused on perception, execution, and imitation of goal-directed actions. Building on                                                                                                                                             the CHRIS project, the authors develop a system where robots learn to observe and perform novel actions in a flexible manner. The architecture uses perceptual primitives (visibility, motion, contact)                                                                                                                                             to recognize actions and motor primitives (grasp, release, touch, look-at) for execution. Actions are represented symmetrically for both perception and execution, enabling imitation. The system is                                                                                                                                             demonstrated across three different robot platforms (two iCub variants and Jido), validating platform independence. Five experiments show: completing perception with execution for known actions,                                                                                                                                             learning new actions like 'cover' and 'uncover', transferring learned actions between robots, incorporating agency detection via Kinect, and using combined perception-execution representations for                                                                                                                                             imitation. The work lays the foundation for open-ended cooperation through shared plans.

title: Dialogue in situated environments: A symbolic approach to perspective-aware grounding, clarification and reasoning for robots
venue: Workshop Grounding Human-Robot Dialog for Spatial Tasks - RSS
summary: This paper presents the DIALOGS module, a natural language processing component for grounding verbal interaction in situated human-robot environments. The system                                                                                                                                             proposes a knowledge-oriented architecture where perceptions from different viewpoints are converted into symbolic facts stored in separate cognitive models, enabling perspective-aware dialogue.                                                                                                                                             DIALOGS has three main stages: parsing (grammar-free, rule-based extraction of sentence structure), interpretation (concept resolution, content analysis, and statement building), and verbalization.                                                                                                                                             The interpretation module handles pronoun/anaphora resolution, noun phrase grounding through ontology queries, and verb resolution via thematic roles. A case study demonstrates the system in a                                                                                                                                             scenario where two humans interact with the Jido robot while packing items. Key features include implicit disambiguation through visual perspective-taking (using the human's viewpoint to resolve                                                                                                                                                 ambiguous references), explicit disambiguation through verbal interaction and pointing gestures, and integration with the ORO ontology server for knowledge management.

title: What are you talking about? Grounding dialogue in a perspective-aware robotic architecture
summary: This paper introduces DIALOGS, a component for natural language interpretation that relies on structured symbolic models of the world to ground verbal interaction in a                                                                                                                                                 perspective-aware robotic architecture. The system processes three categories of human utterances: statements (informational content), desires/orders (intentional content), and questions on                                                                                                                                                 declarative knowledge. The grounding process involves parsing, interpretation (resolution, content analysis, statement building), and verbalization. The paper provides detailed technical analysis                                                                                                                                                 through three examples: extracting informational content from 'The yellow banana is big,' processing intentional content from 'Give me the banana' using thematic roles (performedBy, actsOnObject,                                                                                                                                                     receivedBy), and handling ambiguity requiring clarification when multiple objects match a description. The system queries the ontology to disambiguate references, using discrimination routines to                                                                                                                                                     identify distinguishing features and generate clarification questions. Additional strategies include visual perspective-taking, dialogue history, and gesture recognition to resolve references without                                                                                                                                                     explicit questioning.

title: Modular Open Robots Simulation Engine: MORSE
summary: This paper presents MORSE (Modular Open Robots Simulation Engine), an open-source robotics simulator built on top of Blender's 3D modeling and Game Engine capabilities.                                                                                                                                                     MORSE features a component-based architecture for simulating sensors, actuators, and robots with variable levels of abstraction, from realistic sensor data to abstract high-level commands. It follows                                                                                                                                                     a 'Software-in-the-Loop' philosophy where the evaluated software runs unmodified, making the transition from simulation to real experiments transparent. MORSE is middleware-independent, supporting                                                                                                                                                     YARP, ROS, Pocolibs, and raw sockets through a hook mechanism. The modular design allows assembling robots from reusable components (sensors, actuators, robot platforms, scenes, and modifiers for                                                                                                                                                         adding noise). The paper describes several use cases including multi-robot search and rescue (ROSACE project), heterogeneous robot cooperation with UAVs and UGVs (ACTION project), and helicopter                                                                                                                                                         target tracking (ReSSAC). MORSE supports simulation of multiple robots simultaneously and provides realistic 3D environments with Bullet physics.

title: Anchoring interaction through symbolic knowledge
summary: This extended abstract presents an approach for anchoring human-robot interaction through symbolic knowledge. The work shows how extraction, representation, and use of                                                                                                                                                         symbolic knowledge from real-world perception and verbal/non-verbal interaction enables a grounded and shared model of the world suitable for dialogue understanding, task planning, and reactive                                                                                                                                                         supervision. The anchoring process relies on the situated and embodied nature of human-robot interactions. Three cognitive functions are integrated: (1) physical environment modeling and spatial                                                                                                                                                         reasoning for building symbolic beliefs from geometric data; (2) knowledge representation and management using the ORO platform with independent models per agent enabling perspective-taking; and (3)                                                                                                                                                         dialogue input processing for natural language parsing and concept anchoring. Experiments include the 'Odd One Out' task on the Rosie robot, the 'Spy Game' on the Jido robot, and a 'Moving to London'                                                                                                                                                         daily life scenario. The system is deployed on three distinct robotic platforms demonstrating hardware-agnosticity.

title: ORO, a Knowledge Management Module for Cognitive Architectures in Robotics
summary: This paper introduces ORO (OpenRobots Ontology), an embeddable knowledge management platform for cognitive robotics architectures. ORO is built as a socket-based server on                                                                                                                                                         top of RDF triple storage using the Jena framework and Pellet reasoner for OWL Description Logic reasoning. It features the OpenRobots Common Sense Ontology aligned with OpenCyc, providing a shared                                                                                                                                                         vocabulary of concepts and predicates for human-robot interaction. Key features include: an events framework for reactive supervision triggered by knowledge changes, independent cognitive models for                                                                                                                                                         each agent (supporting perspective-taking and theory of mind), categorization algorithms for computing discriminants among concepts, and bio-inspired memory profiles (short-term and long-term). The                                                                                                                                                         platform is validated on three different robotic architectures: BERT2 (YARP-based), Rosie (ROS-based), and Jido (Pocolibs-based), through experiments including Point-and-Learn knowledge acquisition,                                                                                                                                                         Odd One Out commonsense reasoning, and the Spy Game for interactive object discrimination.

title: Which One? Grounding the Referent Based on Efficient Human-Robot Interaction
summary: This paper proposes strategies for a robot to identify referents when humans provide ambiguous or incomplete object descriptions during interaction. The approach uses an ontology                                                                                                                                                         (ORO) to store and reason about the robot's knowledge, incorporating multiple types of descriptors: visual perspective-taking (computing object visibility from each agent's viewpoint), spatial                                                                                                                                                         perspective-taking (qualitative spatial locations relative to agents using egocentric and addressee-centered frames of reference), symbolic location descriptors (isIn, isOn, isNextTo relations                                                                                                                                                             computed from 3D geometry), and feature descriptors (color, size, shape, type). A clarification algorithm iteratively refines object descriptions by finding the best discriminant among candidate                                                                                                                                                             objects and querying the human when needed. Two validation tasks are presented: a daily-life 'Which one?' scenario where the robot resolves ambiguous requests using perspective-taking and                                                                                                                                                             feature-based disambiguation, and a Spy Game where the robot guesses objects through strategic questioning. The system is tested on Jido and HRP-2 platforms.

title: Towards a Platform-Independent Cooperative Human-Robot Interaction System: I. Perception
summary: This paper presents a platform-independent cooperative human-robot interaction system focused on perception, specifically an online learning method for recognizing simple                                                                                                                                                             human actions related to object manipulation. The CHRIS architecture abstracts platform-specific perceptual details at the lowest level, characterizing actions through perceptual primitives of                                                                                                                                                             motion, contact, and visibility. The system uses an EgoSphere for dynamic object position storage, a Primitive Detection module for extracting perceptual events, and an Action Recognition module that                                                                                                                                                             matches sequences of primitives against known action definitions. The ORO (Open Robot Ontology) knowledge base provides the semantic layer, storing facts as RDF triples with an OpenRobots                                                                                                                                                             Common-Sense Ontology. Spoken language interaction via the CSLU Toolkit enables human teaching of actions. The architecture is validated on two platforms: the iCubLyon01 humanoid using vision-based                                                                                                                                                             Spikenet perception, and the BERT2 robot using VICON motion capture. Knowledge about recognized actions transfers directly between the different robots.

title: Robot, tell me what you know about...?: Expressing robot's knowledge through interaction
summary: This paper presents mechanisms for communicating a robot's internal knowledge to human partners during face-to-face interaction. The approach uses ORO (OpenRobot Ontology), an                                                                                                                                                             RDF/OWL-based knowledge repository that stores symbolic representations of the world, including per-agent cognitive models. A geometric reasoning engine built on Move3D computes perspective-taking                                                                                                                                                             descriptors (visibility, spatial relations, reachability) and symbolic location descriptors (IsIn, IsOn, IsNextTo) from both the robot's and human's viewpoints. A decisional reasoner interprets human                                                                                                                                                             queries, identifies referred objects using discriminant properties, and answers questions about object location, visibility, and reachability. The system supports learning unknown objects through                                                                                                                                                             interactive dialogue. The work combines symbolic knowledge representation with geometric reasoning to enable transparent, explainable robot behavior in collaborative settings.

title: GenoM3: Building middleware-independent robotic components
summary: This paper introduces GenoM3, a new version of the GenoM component generator for building middleware-independent robotic software components. The key innovation is decoupling                                                                                                                                                             the algorithmic core of components from their middleware encapsulation through automatically generated 'glue' code and component templates. Components are defined via a formal description language                                                                                                                                                             (using IDL-based syntax) specifying codels, services, data ports, and execution tasks. Activities within services are modeled as Petri nets governing codel execution. Templates, which are generic                                                                                                                                                             skeletons implementing middleware-specific aspects (threads, communication, marshalling), are instantiated per component via code generation. This approach allows the same algorithmic library to be                                                                                                                                                             used with different middlewares (Pocolibs, YARP, OpenRTM) without modification. The paper also describes two use cases: an OpenRobots simulator built on the Blender Game Engine, and real-time robotic                                                                                                                                                             components.

title: Taught by Children: A New Role for Robots?
venue: Journées Nationales de la Robotique Interactive
summary: This paper presents the first known robotic partner that children can teach handwriting, using the learning by teaching paradigm. A NAO robot uses simulated handwriting on                                                                                     a synchronized tablet, with PCA-based statistical shape models generating purposefully deformed letters that improve through children's demonstrations. Preliminary studies at two Geneva-area schools                                                                                     with over 50 children aged 6-8 validated the approach: children believed the simulated writing was real, engaged in teaching (averaging 10.9 demonstrations per session), and all children believed                                                                                     the robot was learning from them. The system operated autonomously for 160 minutes total, writing 335 letters with only three technical failures. The paper argues this work has impact for both AI                                                                                     (novel learning algorithms for handwriting) and HRI (a new role for robots in education). The learning by teaching paradigm is highlighted as powerful because it engages children as teachers,                                                                                     stimulating meta-cognition and self-esteem, with robots uniquely suited to play naive learners that neither adults nor peers can convincingly portray.

title: Am I capable yet?
summary: This position paper argues that two underexplored prerequisites must be addressed to advance cognitive architectures for HRI: better evaluation metrics for robotic cognition,                                                                                     and improved amodal representations of the robot's environment. For evaluation, the author notes that current assessment relies mostly on qualitative methods and proposes developing reproducible                                                                                     metrics drawing from AI tests, cognitive psychology (e.g., Theory of Mind, False-Belief experiments), and field experiments. The concept of 'cognitive load' is introduced as a global measurement                                                                                     based on interaction frequency between software modules and a central knowledge base. For representations, the paper emphasizes the need for amodal, modality-independent situation assessment that                                                                                     accounts for both physical and mental features of humans, including beliefs, desires, and capabilities. The importance of context management is highlighted, arguing robots need to handle multiple                                                                                     co-existing temporal, spatial, social, and cultural contexts simultaneously, akin to context-dependent memory in cognitive psychology.

title: On Robot Decisional Abilities for Human-Robot Joint Action
summary: This poster presents an integrative approach to robot decisional abilities for human-robot joint action. The work addresses the question of how a robot should perform tasks in                                                                                                                                                             the presence of or in interaction with humans, emphasizing efficiency, safety, acceptability, and intentionality. The approach is plan-based, incorporating on-line deliberation, reasoning,                                                                                                                                                             anticipation, and pro-active behavior, along with Theory of Mind for predicting human activity and mental states. The robot decisional architecture includes an execution controller, human-aware task                                                                                                                                                             planning, and geometric and temporal reasoning layers built on top of a sensorimotor perception layer. Key decisional ingredients include situation assessment, human activity recognition, perspective                                                                                                                                                             taking, and task planning. The work aims to build 'good' plans that ensure legibility and acceptability of robot actions while complying with social conventions.

title: Networking Needs and Solutions for Road Vehicles at Imara
summary: This paper surveys the networking methods developed by the INRIA Imara team to provide a stable communication environment for driverless vehicles (cybercars). It                                                                                                                                                             addresses three use cases: crossroads management (V2I communication), trajectory exchange between vehicles (V2V), and semantic networking using ontologies. The communication architecture is based on                                                                                                                                                             IPv6 with embedded 4G Cube routers providing OLSR mesh networking, Zeroconf/mDNS for automatic naming, DNS-SD for service discovery, and PIM-SM for IP multicast routing. A cybercar ontology written                                                                                                                                                             in OWL provides semantic interoperability, with an ontology server on each vehicle accessible via XML-RPC. The paper presents hardware implementation details, bandwidth measurements showing low                                                                                                                                                             overhead from OLSR and mDNS protocols, and live experiment results demonstrating the viability of the mesh network approach for inter-vehicle communication.

title: SqueakBot: a Pedagogical Robotic Platform
bibtex:
@conference{stinckwich2007squeakbot,
title={{SqueakBot: a Pedagogical Robotic Platform}},
author={Stinckwich, S. and Lemaignan, S. and Saidani, S.},
booktitle={Proceedings of the Fifth International Conference on Creating, Connecting and Collaborating through Computing},
pages={137--144},
year={2007},
organization={IEEE Computer Society}}
summary: SqueakBot is a pedagogical robotics platform developed by Planete Sciences and the University of Caen, designed to teach young people robotics through hands-on                                                                                                                                                             construction and programming. The platform combines simplified electronic control modules (MEC, SMEC, ASPIC, MOEBUS) connected via the I2C protocol with the Squeak/Smalltalk programming environment                                                                                                                                                             and its visual EToys interface, replacing the previously used LOGO language. A Squeak plugin handles low-level parallel port communication with the hardware modules. The platform also includes a                                                                                                                                                             robot simulation system based on the ODE physics engine for Braitenberg vehicle experiments. The paper reports on successful testing with 9 teenagers (ages 13-15) at a 2005 summer camp, where                                                                                                                                                             children built four different robots (Mars explorer, walking robot, autonomous buggy, caterpillar) and learned programming through Squeak's visual, object-oriented environment.

title: La Boite à Bots : quand la robotique s'ouvre à tous
summary: This French-language article presents 'La Boite a Bots,' an educational robotics toolkit developed by Planete Sciences and the University of Caen. The toolkit is                                                                                                                                                             organized around Squeak, an interactive and free Smalltalk environment, along with electronic modules for controlling small robots from a computer. The article explains the value of robotics in                                                                                                                                                             science education using a Perceive-Reason-Act model, and presents three interconnected chains of educational robotics: environmental (mental to physical), logical (idea to physical signals), and                                                                                                                                                             physical (human to physical world). It discusses the constructivist pedagogical foundations inspired by Piaget, Papert, and Minsky, and the choice of Squeak/EToys over LOGO for programming. The                                                                                                                                                             toolkit includes the SqueakBot programming environment, electronic components (SMEC, ASPIC cards), and activity guides, all released under open-source licenses to maximize accessibility.

title: MASON: A proposal for an ontology of manufacturing domain
venue: IEEE Workshop on Distributed Intelligent Systems (DIS)
summary: This paper proposes MASON (MAnufacturing's Semantics ONtology), an upper ontology for the manufacturing domain, formalized using the Web Ontology Language (OWL). The                                                                                                                                                             ontology addresses the need for a common semantic framework to describe manufacturing processes, enabling data formalization and sharing across heterogeneous environments. MASON is built upon three                                                                                                                                                             head concepts: entities (geometric features, raw materials, cost entities), operations (manufacturing, logistic, human operations), and resources (machine-tools, tools, human and geographic                                                                                                                                                                 resources), comprising approximately 270 base concepts and 50 properties. The paper discusses OWL's suitability for manufacturing ontologies, including classes, properties, and restrictions. Two                                                                                                                                                                 applications are presented: automatic cost estimation using expert systems that reason over the ontology to estimate production costs, and a mapper linking OWL ontologies with the JADE multiagent                                                                                                                                                                 framework for manufacturing simulation.
